Hmm, never thought of the need for shell built-ins (most systems running bash-like envs provide most of the useful built-ins as real commands too: eg `/bin/cd` in addition to the `cd` shell built-in.

My main reason for not wanting to do this is that it'll screw up quoting expectations. The way it is now, `ntfy done` is best suited for running single commands. Adding this would allow you to do something like: 
```
ntfy done "echo \"foo\" && echo \"bar\" &> /some/file"
```
but that can also be achieved now by
```
ntfy done bash -c "echo \"foo\" && echo \"bar\" &> /some/file"
```
and it's clear that you need to escape for bash, but if ntfy is doing it for you, it could lead to confusing IMO.

Because I wish to properly package vint for my distro. And that extlib is getting installed directly to site-packages/ dir, because it is not under vint/ dir. Same for compat/, test/, and dev_tool/, but I can avoid installing them, while extlib/ is a requirement for the normal vint functioning.

This is a bigger problem with `find_packages()` in setup.py and your project structure. `find_packages()` without arguments grabs all packages in the tree and with your project layout not all vint related packages are under vint/ dir.

`setuptools` docs suggest using find_packages() for packages with a certain structure. See examples from docs: https://github.com/twisted/twisted and http://svn.eby-sarna.com/PEAK/

The problem with installing vint related files not under vint/ dir is not only a possibility for file collisions, but also leads to problems during import, when several packages install files under say test/ dir in site-packages or extlib/.

Is it a good idea to automate wrap all for loops in with block if the iterable is also a context manager?

make for loop:

```python
for x in gen:
    ...
```
equal to:

```python
with gen:
    for x in gen:
        ...
```
No need to add new `__(a)iterclose__` slot.

An example usage:

```python
for line in open('/some/file.txt'):
    ...
# the file automatic closed after the for block
```
typically, the point of `all`/`gather` is to have them (to know)  all finished. So the when `async with gather` returns you wouldn't need an async for, they should have the result.

Okay, yeah, so the problem was that I only (a) looked at the docs, (b) the source code (but missed the `__getattr__`, and (c) used ipython's interactive help facilities to check for `curio.io.Socket.shutdown` because I was too lazy to actually set up a coroutine to get access to a `Socket` instance. And of course that didn't work, because `__getattr__` only works for lookups on an instance, not on the class. Checking now I can confirm that if I do actually instantiate a `Socket` and try to call `.shutdown` then it works.

I guess it would be nice if `help(curio.io.Socket.shutdown)` and tab completion and such worked -- `__getattr__` doesn't really provide a full replacement. Also if you insist that `close` be async, then you should probably make `shutdown` async too for consistency :-). But these are more minor. Sorry for the confusion!

I think the issue might've been caused by using the wrong alphabet. For instance, in he last example you instantiate `datrie.Trie` with `u"<s>". The `text`, however, contains  other characters as well.

Try this:

```python
>>> t = datrie.Trie(string.printable)
>>> text = u'foo bar'.split()
>>> prev = u'<s>'
>>> for word in text:
...     t[prev] = word
...     prev = word
...
>>> t.items()
[('<s>', 'foo'), ('foo', 'bar')]
```

Indeed I can confirm that using a chunked index (32GB) and piped streams results in undefined behavior.

With:
```
(seqtk mergepe read1.fq.gz read2.fq.gz; cat single.fq.gz) | minimap2 -ax sr db.mmi -
```
minimap2 segfaults once it reaches the second chunk of `db.mmi`.

This is a known complication. See the last paragraph in [this section](https://github.com/lh3/minimap2#map-long-splice) for details. The culprit is human/mouse/yeast use slightly different donor/acceptor motifs from SIRV. The default minimap2 setting is tuned for real biological data but it becomes worse for SIRV. To achieve higher accuracy on SIRV, you should use
```sh
minimap2 -ax splice --splice-flank=no --secondary=no
```
This reverts to the minmap2-2.1.1 behavior.

Haze
brume sèche

The part of the code that you touched basically controls the *appearance* of the diff, but not which rows are considered equal.  If the problems you are seeing are cases where you believe two rows should be considered the same row, because of float equality, then there is more work to be done for that case.

Equality of rows is decided via a hashing process, since an N-to-N comparison would be too slow for non-trivial tables.  In coopy.Index methods toKey and toKeyByContent, a single row of the table is converted to a key which is used to align rows between tables.  You'll see that whitecase and lowercase options insinuate themselves here. Many possible indexes for different choices of columns included are built up and thrown away or combined during the alignment process.

Before working on figuring out how to muscle floating point stuff in there, let me just ask if this sounds like in fact the issue that is affecting you, or is it something else?

@detly  Could you close the issue in that case?

The problem you're facing is that LDAP protocol doesn't allow empty attributes. So when you receive '0' this means that your AD server returns 0, while when you receive [] it means that nothing has been sent by the server. You can avoid the empty list with ``return_empty_attributes=False`` but in this case you must check for the presence of the attribute before requesting its value. 
The return_empty_attributes has been added to avoid cluttering your code with ``if hasattr(entry['attributes']['my:attribute']: ...`` conditions, because only few attributes are mandatory in ldap.

In your case you can just use  ``if not entry['attributes']['lastlogon']: ...`` that catches both (0 and []) conditions or revert the ``return_emtpy_attributes`` to False.

If you haven't already, take a look at the "Explicit Interface Specification" section from the txdbus documentation: http://pythonhosted.org/txdbus/

The line you reference where "Method('exampleMethod'...)" is called is simply defining the signature of the DBus method. You actually invoke the remote method via "proxy_obj.callRemote("exampleMethod"...)". The callRemote() method will return a Twisted Deferred to the eventual results of the call and the addCallback/addErrback methods on the deferred instance are what you're looking for. A good handle on Deferreds is essential for the use of Twisted so you'll want to brush up on that documentation if you're fuzzy on the details.

Original comment by: Andres Riancho

Hi Alan. Thanks for the patches and sorry this wasn't addressed sooner.

After reviewing this patch in detail, I think I'm going to reject it. It suggests some good ideas, but it has a few issues.

* First, the patch is stale and conflicts with some refactoring already done that's very similar (replacing the module resolution with a function). This, of course, was not your fault but ours.
* The patch attempts to do too much (especially for a diff). It would be better to make several changes in successive commits in a pull request.
* While it borrows from another project for resolving the module, I feel the twisted implementation is also deprecated at this point. I'd prefer to use 'importlib' now.
* The patch removes checks for around error handling (the try/except block for ConfigParser errors), but there's no comment indicating why this change is relevant.
* The patch removes support for importing modules relative to the config path name.
* Finally, while the patch description indicates that functions will 'crash' with a NameError, if I understand correctly, the will continue to crash, just with an AttributeError.

I agree there are improvements to be made here, so I don't want to be too discouraging. I do encourage you to continue to contribute. As you do, please try to file tickets with a specific issue, describe that issue (how it's encountered and what you expect to happen differently), and a specific patch or pull request that addresses that issue. Including tests that demonstrate the undesirable behavior and are fixed by the patch are also encouraged.

Thanks for your quick reply, I'm going to try to find a way that satisfies your three valid concerns:

1) I'll use an object internal `dict()` to keep the sockets, the keys will be the thread's TIDs
2) I will not loop or sleep in the Statsd client. I will rather call `connect()` in `send()` instead of `init()` if the socket is not already connected. If an open connection has died I'll try to reconnect only once on `send()`. If a connect fails due to `connection refused` or `connection timed out` the exception will be raised to be handled by the user, so if the user uses TCP he should wrap the `send()` in a `try: except:`.
3) To improve readability I'll split `_send()` into `_send_udp()` and `_send_tcp()`.

What do you think about these ideas?

```python
s = Schema({
        Optional('start'): Use(int),
        basestring: object
    })

s.validate({'start': '1'})
{'start': '1'}   # matched basestring: object
```
`'1'` is not converted to `int` because `basestring` matches before `Optional('start')`

As already pointed out by @apieceofredcloth, I would suggest to increase the priority of the `hasattr(s, 'validate')` case.

Another solution may be to have the classes derived from `Schema` (such as `Optional`) expose the same priority of the underlying schema, so `Optional('start')` would have a priority of `1`, while `Optional(str)` would have a priority of `3`.

I ran a test comparing `send_config_set` with `send_config_set_fast`. 

In the test, I used a script to test how long it took to write a 255 line access-list on a Cisco IOS device. The results of the test are:

# TL;DR Results
`send_config_set`: 142 seconds
`send_config_set_fast`: 30 seconds

# Script
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

import time
from getpass import getpass
from netmiko import ConnectHandler

username = input('Username: ')
password = getpass('Password: ')
enable = getpass('Enable: ')

device = {
    "device_type": "cisco_ios",
    "host": "172.16.1.4",
    "username": username,
    "password": password,
    "secret": enable,
    "verbose": True,
}

acl_name = "ip access-list extended TEST"
acl_config = ["  permit ip host 10.0.0.{} any".format(i) for i in range(255)]
acl_config.insert(0, acl_name)

with ConnectHandler(**device) as ssh:
    ssh.enable()

    input("Starting send_config_set_fast test. Press enter to continue")
    start = time.time()
    o = ssh.send_config_set_fast(acl_config)
    end = time.time()
    elapsed = end - start
    print("Elapsed Time: {}".format(elapsed))
    print(o)

    input("Press Enter to reset test")
    ssh.send_config_set("no {}".format(acl_name))
    o = ssh.send_command("show ip access-list TEST")
    print(o)
    input("Test reset. Press enter to continue")
```

Hey @sivananth,

Thanks for the feedback. 

The code is basically looking at the last date (2015-03-31) and subtracting 3 years from it. This leaves us with the date 2012-03-31 - let's call it t0. Once it has that date, it takes a slice of the initial price series from t0 until the end representing the last 3 years of data. 

In this case, it appears that 2012-03-31 was not a trading day, so the last day of that month was actually 2012-03-30. Therefore, 2012-03-30 is not included in the filtered price series and the numbers are off since the first day in the slice is 2012-04-30.

This issue is tricky to solve. If we knew we always had monthly data, it would be easy to fix, but this is not the case. The code must remain flexible to support any kind of frequency and as of now, this frequency is unknown. 

I'm open to ideas on how to solve this issue. You can look at the code in ```PerformanceStats._update``` for a better idea on how this all works.

Hope this helps,
Phil

> I wouldn't presume that people don't want to SIR sort per frame though.

But if a different permutation were computed for one frame, that would artificially buff the results by assuming a different correlation between the references and estimates than previous frames. Changing the `compute_permutation` parameter to default `True` could lead to a "blind" user (not reading the code/documentation) accepting the measures when they are incorrect.

A user can still pass `compute_permutation = True` and sort every frame, but it requires them accepting they will need to verify no change in permutation across all frames.

> Since for the separation evaluation of long audio tracks the FFTs to calculate are very long, any kind of optimisation here would speed up the BSS evaluation significantly.

> What about adding a time tolerance to the voicing measures for melody extraction?

> Couldn't we use this from the onset module?

Not really; matching events is intended for the case where you have a list of event times, e.g. `[1., 1.1., 1.5, 10., 13.]` and voicing is represented as a boolean array, e.g. `[0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1]`.

The `time()` implementation is already using straight C functions to create a timestamp. Depending on your OS it's basically `create Python Float from gettimeofday`. I'm afraid there is nothing to optimize here.

> How can you use that from _ps*.py modules if it's defined in __init__.py?

It's a matter of import order. If you define it in `__init__` before importing the platform-specific modules, they will succeed in importing from `__init__`. Maybe not the best way to do it though.


> BSD systems do not rely on /proc.

`_psbsd.py` has a few `readlink`s and `open`s from `/proc` (the C extension doesn't, but still..)

I think this solves the current issue at hand.

There's a larger overarching issue which is inconsistent use of TCHAR, char[], WCHAR, etc.

From my own testing ([gist](https://gist.github.com/MechaLynx/e796896197d01a3c3528ac42c1a43751)) it seems `disk_io_counters` returns `read_time` and `write_time` in tens of microseconds, not actual microseconds. To match `perfmon`'s values (`LogicalDisk\Avg. Disk sec/read` for example, check the `Last` indicator) you need to divide by 1e5.

I checked the implementation in `psutil` and I found this to be the commit that implemented `disk_io_counters`: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/commit/199d109c2c642386f23c45ea64a3ee46d681058d

You can see that it multiplies the value by 10 before dividing by 1000. The implementation of that particular value fetch is still the same. The commit doesn't have any message to explain why this is done, but I'm guessing it's to make the value a proper integer instead of a float.

I tested it locally (Win7 pro x64) and neither `read_time` nor `write_time` ever use their last digit, it's always zero. So that multiplication by 10 might have been necessary in the past, but it apparently serves no purpose anymore, at least on Win7.

I would recommend either changing the documentation to indicate that on Windows the returned values are in tens of microseconds (so that the code doesn't need to be modified if it works on other systems) or removing the x10 and stating that it returns microseconds on Windows. I'm assuming milliseconds are returned on other systems so this has to be documented for Windows explicitly.

My point for the first bullet is twofold:

* `remove` could be called from somewhere else in the future where for example the file existence wouldn't be tested by the calling function. In that case I'd rather have a `remove` that returns a boolean telling if the file has been remove or not than an exception which could entirely stop/crash the program.
* `os.unlink` or `rmtree` might fail (think about permission issues, race conditions, etc) thus it'd be useful to return something like `os.path.exists(path)` and let the caller know if effectively the file has been deleted. Also enclosing those calls within try...catch would avoid any crash for the reasons above.

For those reasons I'd rather not raise exceptions from `remove`. Does it make more sense ?

Sorry, it looks like this is a limitation of Docker itself. The entrypoint and environment are only created on the runtime process of the container. Any subsequent ``exec`` calls are new processes, and don't get the same entrypoint, etc. It doesn't seem like there's a built-in way to easily attach to the runtime process with the same settings.

The only real way to be able to test the runtime behavior (env, etc.) of a container would be to issue the test command with the ``docker run``, rather than launching a container in the background and issuing ``docker exec``s against it. 

So I guess this particular issue could be closed as written, but I wonder if there's room for a feature request to be able to issue test commands via ``docker run`` (new container for each command) rather than ``docker exec``.

I ended up being able to solve my issue by modifying the `__enter__` function to reset the calls queue after sleeping, so the call back was called exactly 3 times in 90 requests.s

```
 def __enter__(self):
        with self._lock:
            # We want to ensure that no more than max_calls were run in the allowed
            # period. For this, we store the last timestamps of each call and run
            # the rate verification upon each __enter__ call.
            if len(self.calls) >= self.max_calls:
                until = time.time() + self.period - self._timespan
                if self.callback:
                    t = threading.Thread(target=self.callback, args=(until,))
                    t.daemon = True
                    t.start()
                sleeptime = until - time.time()
                if sleeptime > 0:
                    time.sleep(sleeptime)
                    self.calls = collections.deque()
            return self

```

This was recently fixed and will be included in the next release. Units such as hours are only picked out as standalone words like *792 hours* or attached to the number like *792h*. "792 href" in your example will no longer parse as a date. Since the parsing is done with regular expressions, the word boundary `\b` is used to ensure that the keyword is not just a substring in a larger word.

There were a lot of cases like this, we should be in much better shape now!

First, the `addrFamily` idea is very appealing, thank you!

The 6-octet length is ambiguous between Ethernet and BACnet IPv4 addresses, and yes, it just makes a guess that when the last two octets happen to be BACnet UDP sockets the first four are probably IPv4 address.  You are also correct that the address instance construction comes from the bottom layers going upstream and in those cases setting the family is easy.  When the addresses come from the top of the stack going downstream they are quite often from a database or configuration file and represented as strings so I wanted the parsing code to be as flexible as possible without being ambiguous.

I _was_ thinking that the `IPv4Address` would absorb the parsers out of the `Address` class, and now I think that might be too much.  An IPv6 address tuple `('fe80::32ed', 47808)` needs to be distinguished from an IPv4 address tuple `('1.2.3.4', 47808)` so maybe those two can be specialized subclasses and set the address family as you suggested.  To follow the current design pattern, an IPv6 `addrAddr` octet string would be 18 octets long, the first 16 are the 128-bit IPv6 address and the last two as the port.

Because the BVLL encoding and decoding is quite different, not only is the header a different size but there are more PDU types and the PDU type code for the same "function" is different (like `OriginalBroadcastNPDU`).  So I was anticipating moving the `udp`, `bvll`, and `bvllservice` modules into a subpackage called `ipv4` and then building a new subpackage called `ipv6` with similar modules.  The existing modules would turn into the python version of "look over there" in one version, then have a deprecation warning, then be pulled out.  The two different UDP modules, `bacpypes.ipv4.udp` and `bacpypes.ipv6.udp` would have two different `unpack_ip_addr()` functions.

Packets going upstream from the IPv6 UDPDirector to the corresponding BVLL service objects would have these huge octet strings and I'm assuming that all of the VMAC binding happens at that layer and going upstream from that to the network layer is the 3-octet VMAC address.  What gets presented at the application layer is an open question, I'm guessing that the VMAC address for a device will be stored as an additional attribute in the `DeviceInfo` object that will be shared between the application and BVLL layers.

Maybe I'm wandering too far off track for this issue, but I will poke at this `addrFamily` concept some more.

Yep, this is not very clear.  First, building a `LogRecord` should be somewhat familiar:
```
>>> from bacpypes.primitivedata import Date, Time
>>> from bacpypes.basetypes import DateTime, LogRecord, LogRecordLogDatum

>>> lrld = LogRecordLogDatum(realValue=75.3)
>>> dt = DateTime(date=Date().now().value, time=Time().now().value)
>>> lr1 = LogRecord(timestamp=dt, logDatum=lrld)
>>> lr1.debug_contents()
    timestamp
        date = (118, 8, 11, 6)
        time = (23, 12, 37, 43)
    logDatum
        realValue = 75.3
```
The `Any` can take any contsructed data type, but this is a specific data type which we need to build first:
```
>>> from bacpypes.constructeddata import SequenceOf, Any
>>> 
>>> SequenceOfLogRecord = SequenceOf(LogRecord)
>>> solr = SequenceOfLogRecord([lr1])
```
So now `solr` is an instance of a sequence of log records.  Now build the `ReadRangeACK` and put in a placeholder `Any` where the value is going to go, then cast-in the sequence.
```
>>> from bacpypes.apdu import ReadRangeACK
>>> rra = ReadRangeACK(
    objectIdentifier=('analogValue', 1),
    propertyIdentifier='presentValue',
    resultFlags=[1, 0, 1],
    itemCount=1,
    itemData=Any(),
    )
>>> rra.itemData.cast_in(solr)
```
The other end of the service is going to need the same data type to be able to cast it out:
```
>>> z = rrs.itemData.cast_out(SequenceOfLogRecord)
```
The result is _not_ an instance like it was going it (which I guess is a bit
unfortunate) but just a Python list:
```
>>> z
[<bacpypes.basetypes.LogRecord object at 0x7fc7f2ac7850>]
>>> 
>>> z[0].debug_contents()
    timestamp
        date = (118, 8, 11, 6)
        time = (23, 12, 37, 43)
    logDatum
        realValue = 75.30000305175781
```

As far as work-arounds are concerned, you can try to use the file descriptor corresponding to your "pipe" file object:

```Python
sf = soundfile.SoundFile(sp.stdout.fileno())
```

But note that this will *not* work on Windows if you use our packaged DLL (nor with the DLL from the official libsndfile installer), you'd have to use a DLL that's compiled with the same compiler as the Python interpreter itself (see http://www.mega-nerd.com/libsndfile/api.html#open_fd).

Another work-around would be to use a named pipe (e.g. with [os.mkfifo()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.mkfifo)).
Sadly, this won't work on Windows either, because this is only supported for Unix-like systems.

So, I also added a third white list, the one that pheraps is better and easier to solve this kind of problems: `white_list_route`:

```
clients:
  - asn: 11
    ip: "192.0.2.11"
    cfg:
      filtering:
        irrdb:
          as_sets:
            - "AS-AS11NETS"
          white_list_route:
            - prefix: "203.0.113.0"
              length: 24
              asn: 65534
```

The syntax is the same already used for other prefix-list entries, with the addition of an optional `asn` attribute, used to match the origin ASN.

From `clients.yml`:

> The first one, 'white_list_route', allows routes to
> "escape" IRRdb-based filters and to be always accepted.
> Entries of this list are made up by prefixes and an
> optional ASN which, if present, is matched against the
> origin ASN. In this case, routes that pass the IRRdb
> filters are tagged with 'prefix_not_present_in_as_set'
> and 'origin_not_present_in_as_set' accordingly to the
> configured policy.
> 
> The last two, 'white_list_pref' and 'white_list_asn'
> are more "tricky". They allow to treat prefixes and ASNs
> as if they were part of the client's AS-SET.
> This implies that filters and features based on IRRdb
> data are also applied as if routes were validated against
> those data.
> For example, if tagging via BGP community was enabled,
> when a route is accepted because of one or both of these
> white lists then the appropriate
> 'prefix_present_in_as_set' or 'origin_present_in_as_set'
> communities are also added.
> 
> In any case, these white lists only apply to IRRdb-based
> filters; other filters (NEXT_HOP, prefix length, max
> AS_PATH length, transit-free/invalid ASN in AS_PATH and
> so on) are applied accordingly to the rest of the
> configuration.
> 
> Prefixes are always intended as being inclusive of more
> specifics unless the 'exact' attribute is set to True.
> The range of included sub-prefixes can be set using 'ge'
> and 'le' attributes; if they are missing,
> length-[32|128] is used.

Actually, I bet I know why this is - I bet it is because unittest is importing everything so it can discover the tests.

I added a print to the top of `flask_table/__init__.py` and to the top of `tests/__init__.py` and ran both test commands:
```
❯ python setup.py test 2>&1 | grep 'Importing'
Importing tests/__init__.py
Importing flask_table/__init__.py
❯ python -m unittest discover 2>&1 | grep 'Importing'
Importing flask_table/__init__.py
Importing tests/__init__.py
```
So discover is importing the library before it imports the tests, so the environment variables are not overridden when Babel is imported (Babel configures things at import time), and so it loads based on your local environment.

If you run `python -m unittest tests`, it won't look for the tests in the library code, and - I hope - the tests will pass for you. Or just run `python setup.py tests`.

The file example you described does not adhere to the specifications of the SPEC standard data file format.  Please consult the SPEC manual and construct SPEC data files using the [Standard Data File Format](https://certif.com/spec_manual/user_1_4_1.html).

This is a *fragment* of a SPEC data file.  It only includes the scans but not the header lines of the file.

``spec2nexus`` requires that SPEC data files begin with these four control lines: ``#F``, ``#E``, ``#D``, ``#C``.  Without these control lines, as described in the SPEC standard  data file format, ``spec2nexus`` will not identify this file as a SPEC file and raise the ``NotASpecDataFile`` exception.

A UTF-8 file or stream can contain the BOM sequence (from the same FAQ you linked: [Can a UTF-8 data stream contain the BOM character (in UTF-8 form)?](http://unicode.org/faq/utf_bom.html#bom5 "http://unicode.org/faq/utf_bom.html#bom5")).

You are correct that the BOM has no intrinsic meaning within UTF-8.  When it occurs, its only purpose is to act as a signature, indicating that the file/stream is UTF-8 encoded.  As you've noticed, however, that usage is problematic as it can interfere with software which doesn't expect the signature.

It's use in UTF-8 files/streams is largely a Microsoft-ism.

Since we need the functionality, we're currently (successfully) running a monkeypatched version of the `xlsxwriter.chart_radar.ChartRadar._write_legend` method that looks like this:

```python
def _write_legend(self):
    # Write the <c:legend> element.
    position = self.legend_position
    font = self.legend_font
    delete_series = []
    overlay = 0

    if (self.legend_delete_series is not None
            and type(self.legend_delete_series) is list):
        delete_series = self.legend_delete_series

    if position.startswith('overlay_'):
        position = position.replace('overlay_', '')
        overlay = 1

    allowed = {
        'right': 'r',
        'left': 'l',
        'top': 't',
        'top_right': 'tr',
        'bottom': 'b',
    }

    if position == 'none':
        return

    if position not in allowed:
        return

    position = allowed[position]

    self._xml_start_tag('c:legend')

    # Write the c:legendPos element.
    self._write_legend_pos(position)

    # Remove series labels from the legend.
    for index in delete_series:
        # Write the c:legendEntry element.
        self._write_legend_entry(index)

    # Write the c:layout element.
    self._write_layout(self.legend_layout, 'legend')

    if font:
        self._write_tx_pr(None, font)

    # Write the c:overlay element.
    if overlay:
        self._write_overlay()

So there's going to be a severe layers violation here if we try to do that. The preparation of that multipart body happens well before the betamax adapter sees it. That stuff happens before we even get the data.

There's no way we as a library can do this and I'm not certain what the best fix is. As I see it, we have two options:

1. Users could use the [`MultipartEncoder`](https://toolbelt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/uploading-data.html#streaming-multipart-data-encoder) which allows users to stream large uploads & to select their own boundary.

2. In the betamax-matchers package, we could use the [undocumented](https://github.com/sigmavirus24/requests-toolbelt/issues/71) [`MultipartDecoder`](https://github.com/sigmavirus24/requests-toolbelt/blob/master/requests_toolbelt/multipart/decoder.py#L152) to parse the body and do matching that way.

Personally I'm leaning towards 2. Thoughts @bboe?

**TL;DR:** `try/except` with `session.send()` works, but adds a lot of boilerplate. `retries` approach as in #65 seems OK if opt-in.

-----

Note: `v2.0.0` has been released; I've used it for the experiment below. It has no changes compared to `v2.0.0rc2`.

I've tried the approach from my previous comment. It can be seen in [this gist](https://gist.github.com/veox/fa2a406de0a0dcfda234374fa014e444).

In short - it works. (Tried a few times.) A query that seems to fail with a `502`, but does in fact go through, will be rejected by Kraken when repeated, with a response of:

``` python
{'error': ['EAPI:Invalid nonce']}
```

In other words, the nonce is indeed sufficient for Kraken to recognise it as a duplicate.

-----

This approach, however, adds a lot of boilerplate to the end application. The "wrapper" must be repeated for every query type, either manually or with a decorator.

This is unwieldy, and, more importantly, would likely be too complicated for a lot of people using `krakenex`. (From [numerous issues reported this year](https://github.com/veox/python3-krakenex/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue%20label%3Aquestion%20), I'm guessing trading automation is the first sting with programming for many.)

@gzhytar's approach seems warranted - it doesn't add _too much_ bloat, and _is_ a solution for a common issue, especially in light of Kraken's current (abysmal) performance.

This should be, however, a deliberate opt-in setting, as mentioned in https://github.com/veox/python3-krakenex/pull/65#issuecomment-343757007. Otherwise, it's only likely to further increase the load on Kraken's TEX.

Yeah, but we can only use the stdlib tar/zip libraries if there's a way to make them streaming-friendly:

* On `send`, we need to create a fake file-like object to pass into `zipfile.ZipFile()` for writing. Each time the zipfile object calls `obj.write(data)`, we need to send that data over the wire. If the zipfile object ever calls `obj.seek()`, then we're hosed (and I assume it will want to seek back to the beginning, after writing out all the data, to fill in the directory table).
* On `receive`, the `ZipFile` will be calling `obj.read(length)` on our file-like object, which needs to return the compressed data. But the flow control is going in the other direction: the receiver reacts to new data arriving, rather than asking for data to be input. So we'd either have to run the `ZipFile` in a thread somehow, or write the whole stream to a tempfile (so we can be sure it's all available before `obj.read()` gets called), which uses up just as much disk space as before.

Running them in a subprocess would let us use stdin/stdout as the file-like object, and deal with the flow-control in the pipe that sits between them.

One approach might be to skip the zipfile entirely and send each file, one at a time, along with a header that indicates everything the zipfile would have carried. So the protocol alternates between e.g. length-prefixed JSON blobs (that include SIZE and PATH and MODE), then SIZE bytes of data (maybe compressed). The receiver would write files to a neighboring temporary directory (tmpdir/PATH) as they arrived. The last record would be a JSON blob with a special "I'm done" flag, which would signal the receiver to move the tempdir into place at the final output path.

We'd lose the benefit of compression across files, unfortunately. But it wouldn't need the temporary zipfile.

This is a unique use-case. The issue appears to be caused because the bash-git-prompt code runs after the powerline-shell code. I think what you want to do is change up your `PROMPT_COMMAND` so that powerline-shell runs after bash-git-prompt, but also change the `_update_ps1` function so it does not run when in a git directory. Change the last lines of the above to this:

```
function _update_ps1() {
    local repo=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2> /dev/null)
    if [[ ! -e "$repo" ]] && [[ "$GIT_PROMPT_ONLY_IN_REPO" = 1 ]]; then
        PS1="$(powerline-shell $?)"
    fi
}

if [[ $TERM != linux && ! $PROMPT_COMMAND =~ _update_ps1 ]]; then
    PROMPT_COMMAND="$PROMPT_COMMAND; _update_ps1;"
fi
```

Due to no response from mantainer, I´m going to post how I modified client.py code to incorporate ntlm proxy support, for those people who could get ntlm proxy support with flask-oauthlib.

```
# Notes: (this is only for python3. For python2 you could use ntlm )
# you have to install ntlm support for urllib2 (its whats used inside flask-oauth). 
# pip3 install python-ntlm3
# Could be great change urllib/urllib2 for most modern "request" lib 
# and use "session" instead, to do not make an ntlm auth process for each url request.
from ntlm3 import HTTPNtlmAuthHandler 
```

and inside http_request in client.py module 
```
def http_request(uri, headers=None, data=None, method=None):
        uri, headers, data, method = prepare_request(
            uri, headers, data, method
        )
        
        #from ntlm import HTTPNtlmAuthHandler
        user = 'DOMAIN\username' #domain and username for proxy ntlm 
        password = "pass" #the password
        passman = http.HTTPPasswordMgrWithDefaultRealm()
        passman.add_password(None, 'http://my_proxy:8080', user, password) # substitute http://my_proxy:808 for the url and port of your ntlm proxy
        # create the NTLM authentication handler
        auth_NTLM = HTTPNtlmAuthHandler.HTTPNtlmAuthHandler(passman)  
        # other authentication handlers
        auth_basic = http.HTTPBasicAuthHandler(passman)
        auth_digest = http.HTTPDigestAuthHandler(passman)
        proxy_handler = http.ProxyHandler({'http': 'http://my_proxy:8080', 
                                           'https': 'http://my_proxy:8080'}) # include proxys as default for http and https
        opener = http.build_opener(proxy_handler, auth_NTLM, auth_digest, auth_basic)
        http.install_opener(opener)
        
        # the rest of original code remains the same...
        log.debug('Request %r with %r method' % (uri, method))
        req = http.Request(uri, headers=headers, data=data)
        req.get_method = lambda: method.upper()
        try:
            resp = http.urlopen(req)
            content = resp.read()
            resp.close()
            return resp, content
        except http.HTTPError as resp:
            content = resp.read()
            resp.close()
            return resp, content
```

This code has been sucessfully tested with a corporate ntlm proxy.

Hi,
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/models/fields/#django.db.models.DateField.auto_now
Django respects `auto_now` option only when `save` is called like pre-save and post-save signals. It doesn't automatically update the field when you call `update`. Because `update` is considered a low-level SQL thing and it passes the exact values into db directly without any validations. So same thing actually goes for `bulk-update`, it would be overkill to check all fields for `auto_now` or `default` options.

In short, you can set date values explicitly before you call `bulk-update`.
Ex:
```
now = datetime.datetime.now()
for obj in objs:
    obj.datetime_field = now
```

```python
from exchangelib.folders import ItemAttachment, FileAttachment

def processCompleted():
    folder = account.root.get_folder_by_name('COMPLETED EMAILS')
    tz = EWSTimeZone.timezone('US/Eastern')
    items = folder.filter(datetime_received__gt=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(2016, 12, 12)))
    for idx, message in enumerate(items):
        for attachment in message.attachments:
            if isinstance(attachment, ItemAttachment):
                # Print the attached EWS item, which can be a CalendarItem, a Message, etc
                print(idx, attachment.item)
                continue
            if isinstance(attachment, FileAttachment):
                # Print the contents of the attached file
                print(idx, attachment.content)
                continue
            raise ValueError('Unknown attachment type: %s' % attachment)

Hi,

You can store additional meta information on a revision by creating a
custom metadata model:

class YourRevisionMeta(models.Model):
    revision = models.ForeignKey(Revision)
    view_name = models.CharField(max_length=200)

Then, in your view that is saving models, just call:

reversion.add_meta(YourRevisionMeta, view_name="some_view")

This technique can be adapted to store all sorts of additional meta info.

Simply access instances of YourRevisionMeta from the database in your
on_commit handler to get at the metadata.

I'm assuming you're using Jupyter notebooks from the "dead kernel" speak.  

A Jupyter kernel often dies from resource limitations (e.g. when your program is requesting more memory than has been allocated to the kernel).  Since you're trying to tune batch size with a hyperparameter search, it's highly likely that you're searching values of batch size that require more memory than you've got available.  This is mentioned about Tensorflow specifically [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/41386795/tensorflow-saver-kernel-appears-to-have-died).  I would recommend tuning batch size by hand before using Bayesian optimization.  Common choices for batch size are 32, 64, 128.  It mostly depends on how much data you have and your memory resources, but many people go with defaults of 32 or 64.  

Closing this issue since it has nothing to do with this package specifically.

Basically the problem is that the `glob` function returns bytes-based `str` on Python 2 rather than Unicode. But if you provide Unicode, it returns Unicode as well. So use `ext=u'*.xsl*'` and also provide the names of directories as Unicode. You can also use `from __future__ import unicode_literals`, so `'something'` means `u'something'` by default.

I know about this issue – the problem is direct input rather than output. If you write "Ä", you get '\xc3\x84', which is right. If you write u"Ä", the compiler gets sequence of bytes 'u"\xc3\x84"', which is right, but it should interpret is as u'\xc4' and not u'\xc3\x84'. The interpretation is deduced from some properties of `sys.std*`. The default objects used under Python 2 are carefully designed for this. I don't know why it doesn't work in your case. Try it on vanilla Python installation with just `win-unicode-console` being enabled, no other customization. If it's the same, try to update to Python 2.7.8.

Running the cronjob produces text output (messages etc.). I don't know what system you are running, but chances are that if there is output during the cronjob, that it by default is send as an email to the user that the cronjob is running for (you), so that errors etc. won't go unnoticed.

You might want to redirect the output to a log file. 
`* * * * * ... /home/env/project/bin/python /home/env/project/project/manage.py cron  2>&1 >>chroniker.log`

(Take care, the log will grow over time and should be "rotated")

If you don't care about the output at all, you could redirect it straight to the trash.
`* * * * * ... /home/env/project/bin/python /home/env/project/project/manage.py cron  2>&1 >>/dev/null`

Your mileage may vary.

The problem was that uwsgi, by default, doesn't allow python plugin to initialize the GIL, so this means  that your app-generated threads will not run.
Problem was solved by editing configuration file uwsgi.ini:
Before:
`[uwsgi]
module = application:application

master = true
processes = 5

socket = tradebot.sock
chmod-socket = 666

vacuum = true

die-on-term = true`
After:
`[uwsgi]
module = application:application

processes = 1
enable-threads = true
single-interpreter = true

socket = tradebot.sock
chmod-socket = 666

vacuum = true

die-on-term = true`
The flag "master" causing trouble too. I found explanation [there](https://docs.newrelic.com/docs/agents/python-agent/hosting-mechanisms/python-agent-uwsgi)

> However I don't agree that such libraries should both provide runtime typechecking annotations and an internal API for manual type checking.

The thing is, typechecking annotations would internally need the API for (manual) type checking anyway. So the latter is a by-product of the former or in turn the former is a low-hanging fruit if the latter is given. That's why it makes sense to offer both feature areas within the same framework.

> My latest choice of such tools was enforce

I also saw encforce, typeguard and some more frameworks, but none of them seemed to support stub files, which I find a crucial feature as it unlocks typeshed. (Python 2.7 support is another thing, but I guess this isn't much of interest for you).

I figured it out. I was missing the REFERENCES permission. If anyone else is curious, here's what I've found to be a minimum preferences to get started:

SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, ALTER, CREATE TABLE, REFERENCES, EXEC

...and here's a T-SQL script I've made to easily create users for our Django projects in production:

`/*
This Script Creates a SQL Server Database, Login and User
With Appropriate Permissions for a Production Django Project
with migrations. Simply fill out the variables below (@db_name and @db_password)
Username will be set to database name + '_user' by default.
*/

DECLARE	@db_name VARCHAR(MAX) = 'project'
DECLARE @db_password VARCHAR(MAX) = 'password'
DECLARE @db_user VARCHAR(MAX) = @db_name + '_user'

--
-- 
USE master
DECLARE @cmd VARCHAR(MAX)

-- Server scope: create SQL Server login and permissions
SET @cmd = 'CREATE LOGIN ' + @db_user + ' WITH PASSWORD = ''' + @db_password + ''''
EXEC(@cmd)
SET @cmd = 'GRANT VIEW SERVER STATE TO ' + @db_user
EXEC(@cmd)
SET @cmd = 'CREATE DATABASE [' + @db_name + ']'
EXEC(@cmd)

-- DB scope: create user for server login and permissions
SET @cmd = 'USE [' + @db_name + '];'
SET @cmd = @cmd + 'CREATE USER ' + @db_user + ' FOR LOGIN ' + @db_user + ';'
SET @cmd = @cmd + 'GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, ALTER, CREATE TABLE, REFERENCES, EXEC TO ' + @db_user
EXEC(@cmd)`

It would definitely be considered acceptable, albeit not semantically but practically and with a caveat. My pendantic self will try to explain a bit more, feel free to ignore the rest and just proceed with the change.

The '**ϑ**' (the Greek theta symbol, http://unicode-table.com/en/03D1/) is not really used as a letter (as in I haven't seen a text containing it in prose in my whole life) in Modern Greek, at least in non handwritten text (handwritten text is so subjective, we probably should totally avoid discussing). The letter used in everyday text is '**θ**' (http://unicode-table.com/en/03B8/). The most probable reason for this (and one that is semantically correct) is that Greek keyboard layouts will not emit '**ϑ**' in any easy way but will emit '**θ**'. Writing the symbol form requires either copy pasting, knowing the keyboard unicode key sequence or using a specialized program.

'**ϑ**' is used as a mathematical symbol. While using the letter form ('**θ**') in mathematics is acceptable, some mathematics professors encourage students to distinguish between '**ϑ**'  and '**θ**' (even in handwriting) without however strongly enforcing the distinction (as in penalizing students for not using the correct form).

So, your comment is correct with the caveat that if this library is ever used to transliterate a text (like a mathematical one or even this comment) conveying meaning via the use of the symbol, that meaning will be lost. Which is practically fine, I think.

I have been digging quite alot on this issue over the last couple of weeks. Unfortunately, news are not that great.

**Path 1:**
I have tried overriding the validation process, which is easily done on Django 1.7+. However, none of the changelist options actually work with a translated field, because the inner `ChangeList` object of the admin module does some more introspection.
Now, I tried looking at overriding that, but this object is a huge, complex spaghetti object with an interface that often changes. Not really an option. That path does not look really bright.

**Path 2:**
I tried registering the translated fields as virtual fields onto the shared model, using the new Django 1.8 `_meta` API. It could work. However, doing that has huge implications over all the codebase, as translated fields get seen by third party modules and Django internals (such as ModelForm).

Basically it would be a complete paradigm shift for hvad, from *"hide it to all, then provide language-aware pieces under controller environment"* to *"show fields to all, then selectively override pieces so they act blind"*.
That means refactoring admin, forms and rest support from scratch. And models definition itself of course. Not to mention uncontrollable changes in projects using hvad as translated fields suddenly become visible to all third-party modules.

Actually, implementing path 2 is almost the same as starting a new `hvad2` project, building upon hvad's experience more than hvad's code. Probably using the opportunity to refactor translation queryset as well (I'd love seeing deep queries work). That's huge however, and unlikely to happen soon.

**So?**
Well, so I'm still stuck for most of advanced admin support.
The `add`/`change` form should not be that much of a problem however, and you can expect the `fields`, `fieldsets` and `exclude` options to work in next release. The object list on the other hand is an entirely different beast.

There is no *roadmap*, but #249 and #273 give a pretty good picture of what I would like the future of hvad to be. Especially #273 is a major refactor that would change the way queries are altered, leveraging Django's ORM evolutions to lift the biggest restriction of hvad: its inability to work with joins (`.filter(some_model__translatable_field="foo")`). For filtering it's easy to workaround (manually add an intermediate `__translations` and filter on language code), but it's cumbersome and is plain impossible to do for some other methods.
Also, that would enable reverse managers on relations to translatable models to work seamlessly. And probably solve many issues with admin.

For 1.7.0, I would like to fix the other lingering bugs and suppress the old deprecation exceptions.

You may also install current master using `pip install https://github.com/KristianOellegaard/django-hvad/archive/master.zip`. Right now, it's exactly v1.6.0 with this feature added and bug #319 fixed.

If I understand what you're after correctly, some of the information you're after is in the metadata:

```
% egrep -h "^33" resources/carrier/*/* | awk -F\| '{print $2}' | sort -u
Bouygues
Orange France
SFR
% egrep -h "^41" resources/carrier/*/* | awk -F\| '{print $2}' | sort -u
Orange
Sunrise
Swisscom
Tele4u
```

However, it's not a particularly reliable mapping -- not all countries have number ranges by network operator.

```
% egrep -h "^44" resources/carrier/*/* 
%
```

So I'm not sure that even manually grepping the metadata will get what you want, I'm afraid.

@renato2099 yes.  Keep in mind that the `spark.executor.cores` is a resource allocation hint and not a hardware limit.  It just tells Spark that you only want to run one task at a time on each executor, which in the TFoS setting means that we only want to run one TensorFlow node on each executor.  And this was chosen as the simplest/easiest-to-reason-about "level of abstraction" (vs. on TensorFlow node per task)... for example, each executor's log will only contain log statements from one TensorFlow node.

No, this would waste too many resources. Generally, multiple failures can be fixed by one change. If we wouldn't follow fail-fast, a lot of time would be wasted. Considering the fact that PRs should be small, the chance of multiple independent bugs causing multiple test failures is quite small compared to the number of total tests executed within every PR.

The right approach is to fix one test failure, create a new commit and then fix the next test failure. While this actually sounds pretty wasteful as well, past experience has shown that this is rarely required.

Additionally, we're currently working on something that allows you to run tests locally in exactly the same way the CI does. This will allow you to test yourself before pushing it to the CI.

```

     x, y, z = op()

except:
     handle_exc()
y.asnumpy() #exception_ptr is nullptr, doesn't throw

@piiswrong As depicted in the example above, if we decide to invalidate exception_ptr for y by setting it to nullptr when we WaitToRead x (I am unsure how we will do this), then we won't be propagating exceptions down the chain. Therefore, the last line here will execute just fine instead of throwing an  exception, and user will end up with garbage values for y. 

I understand your point that if an op has multiple write vars, and if we waited for one of the write vars and re-threw exception, we shouldn't throw it again for other vars. But, if we end up invalidating the exception_ptr, any continuing operators may or may not fail, and since the exception_ptr is invalidated we wouldn't be re-throwing the exception in any of the following WaitToReads.

For mxnet, it's up to the user to persist the serialized symbolic graph in a form that can be recognized later.

As for the library that deals with visualization of different symbolic graphs, I think it's the responsibility of the user of that library to specify how a serialized file should be interpreted. After all, it's impossible to tell a json is for which library, if the json serialization results of two libraries are identical but are semantically different. Using filename for such decision is an even worse way to go for obvious reasons...

The advanced indexing I meant is indexing an NDArray with indices containing lists, np.ndarray, NDArray etc., instead of integers or slices. In your example, `a[[0, -1]]` is advanced indexing, and it internally calls `gather_nd` operator which only supports non-negative indices. `a[(0, -1)]` is equivalent to `a[0, -1]` and is basic indexing, which internally calls `slice` operator and it supports non-negative indices.

In your last example, both `xs[(a, b)]` and `xs[a][b]` are basic indexing if `a` and `b` are integers or slices. Something like `a[[0, 1]]` is slower than `a[0, 1]` is because the index `[0, 1]` has to be prepared in Python as an NDArray and passed to the backend operator, while `(0, 1)` has much less overhead to be passed to the backend operator.

You can try the `ctx2group` option during simple_bind(). For example, if you want to specify a layer on cpu among the gpu layers,
```
a = mx.sym.var("a")
b = mx.sym.var("b")
with mx.AttrScope(ctx_group="cpu"):
    c = mx.sym.sample_negative_binomial(a,b) # runs on cpu
d = mx.sym.dot(a, c) # runs on gpu

executor = d.simple_bind(ctx=mx.gpu(), a=(10,), b=(10,), group2ctx={"cpu": mx.cpu()})
```
allows `sample_negative_binomial` to be on cpu while the rest on gpu. 

Caveat: there's a known bug for elemwise_add with group2ctx https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/7080
Certain convolution layouts on CPU are not supported though API claims them to be supported (e.g. NWC NHWC NDHWC).

The input length note being supported means that there's no way for me to enforce consistency between CPU and GPU implementation. cudnn version also doesn't reset the padding area gradients to zero, which can cause instability in training. It's too much of a risk to leave it be.

We don't *pull* from the batch queue in several threads, we *push* to it. On the importers, it's _populate_batch_queue that's the target of the threads.

Here's what I ran to test this:

```python
import time

from threading import Thread
from Queue import Queue
from itertools import cycle

data = [1,2,3,4,5]
data = cycle(data)
queue = Queue()
log_queue = Queue()

def populate_queue(no):
    time.sleep(1)
    count = 0
    for item in data:
        log_queue.put((no, item))
        queue.put(item)
        count = count + 1
        if count == 150:
            break

threads = [Thread(target=populate_queue, args=(i,)) for i in range(4)]
[t.start() for t in threads]
[t.join() for t in threads]

item_ = 0
while not log_queue.empty():
    no, item = log_queue.get()
    print("thread {}, item {}".format(no, item))
```

What's happening is that for some 〈audio file, transcription〉 pair in your dataset, the feature array that gets computed from the audio file is too short, and has less frames than the transcription has characters. In your case this seems particularly extreme, where the transcription has 32 characters and the feature array only has 10 frames. There are two possibilities:

1. The transcription is incorrect, and doesn't match the audio file. See for example [the Fisher importer](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/bin/import_fisher.py#L41). You can either remove them or fix the transcription.
2. The transcription is correct, but the audio file is tiny, usually something like "huh" or "hi". You have two options:
    1. If these are most of your dataset, remove the striding (done in util/audio.py and native_client/deepspeech_utils.cc), and possibly reduce the window step parameter used for the MFCCs.
    2. If these only happen a couple of times, throw them away. (See for example #790)

@francisco-dlp the bouncing behavior would result from the fact that the two traits would always send each other different values from what they already have. Consider the following case of a 2D linked trait relationship:

```
a(b) = b**2
b(a) = a**2
```

Here, `a` and `b` are not related by inverse functions. Thus, if we were to set `a` with the value 2...

+ the change in `a` would cause `b` to update to 4
+ the change in `b` would cause `a` to update to 16
+ the change in `a` would cause `b` to update to 256
+ on and on forever...

The only reason that we don't get infinite recursion in 2D linked traits is when changes in a proverbial trait `b` do not cause a change in trait `a` - the lack of change in `a` is what kills the update notifications.

I've been investigating this issue, and it seems to be SQS's specified behavior. You are not guaranteed to receive 10 messages if there are 10 or more messages (especially with a small number of messages). All that long polling (enabled by setting `WaitTimeSeconds`) guarantees is that all the machines will be searched, and that it won't return empty until the time is up. When you have a large number of messages in the queue you are much more likely to receive 10 messages in a call.

To clarify, the timeout's being associated with an endpoint was [pre-existing](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/endpoint.py#L89).  This PR just plumbs that up to the client configs so users can specify the values.

The endpoint in botocore conceptually represents a remote AWS endpoint you'd send and receive requests to.  So you'd create a request dict, and then use `endpoint.make_request` to send the request to an AWS service.  It also abstracts away (for the most part) an HTTP client.  So given it's the part of botocore responsible for sending/receiving requests (of which a timeout is a property of sending a request), I'm ok with keeping timeout associated with an endpoint object.  I'd be open to hearing alternatives though.

I don't think this is the case in that awscli doesn't write to the cache itself, it merely [tells boto to do so](https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/blob/345df8555b8e5a40bbf2418484458ad9fe8ff77e/awscli/customizations/assumerole.py#L39-L40) so this change in `botocore` would just cause botocore to use the cache (in the same way asserting `credential_cache = True` in the config would do). So no race condition but it would make sense after this is merged to remove the then-redundant code in the awscli `assumerole.py` customization

> If this is accepted, I imagine the awscli cache should be removed.

Yes, all that `awscli` would need to do after this is to remove the [assume role customization](https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/blob/develop/awscli/customizations/assumerole.py) and add an awscli default cache location of `~/.aws/cli/cache`

@allquixotic librespot accesses Spotify using the same method that their desktop/mobile clients do. There is little to no risk that librespot will fail due to endpoints being closed. Re-reading https://github.com/pypa/pip/blob/master/src/pip/_vendor/README.rst showed me that I'd misremembered, and `pip`'s approach does include the ability to apply patches to the vendored pacakges - it's just as a matter of policy, the only patches they apply are the ones to make vendoring work.

So `pipenv` should be able to use the same technical approach, and just apply a different policy as to the kinds of patches which are considered acceptable.

I’ve been testing this with my own installation, and there are a behaviour that might worth discussing. Currently (both on master and here) a Python is chosen with a first-match-wins strategy. Say I want Python 3. The finder first look for one in pyenv (or registry on Windows), and then in PATH. If the user, say, installed only old Pythons in pyenv, there’s no way to get to the new Pythons in PATH.

The alternative would be to collect *all* possible Pythons, so we can choose the best possible match. This is what the Python launcher (`py.exe`) does, but I wonder if it is necessary here.

So the module depends on `GLIBC > 2.5` (as `audiwheel` reports,
but the conclusion of `auditwheel` is that this is OK, which it isn't).


Looking at what versions of GLIBC the undefined symbols require (below), there are two lines for 2.14:

```
107: 0000000000000000      0 FUNC    GLOBAL DEFAULT    UNDEF memcpy@GLIBC_2.14 (4)
1669: 0000000000000000      0 FUNC    GLOBAL DEFAULT    UNDEF memcpy@@GLIBC_2.14
```

So `memcpy` is one of the offending functions. (There is also `__isoc99_sscanf@GLIBC_2.7` and some other symbols, but let us focus on `memcpy`.) As discussed [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35656696/explanation-of-memcpy-memmove-glibc-2-14-2-2-5), `memcpy` in GLIBC_2.7 is not backwards compatible. For completeness, `memcpy` is used by CUDD (which is statically linked in the module that causes trouble) on [`cudd/cuddLCache.c`/Line 247](https://github.com/johnyf/cudd/blob/1bb1ed3de3b00c4c82577027c08e79ae9e9f4a87/cudd/cudd/cuddLCache.c#L247). (CUDD is a C library for decision diagrams, developed over the past 20 years.)

So, it appears that indeed, due to `memcpy` and other symbols, the wheel requires `GLIBC_2.14`, which is reported in the list of requirements by `auditwheel`, however the conclusion by `auditwheel` is that the wheel complies to the `manylinux` tag. This seems to be a bug of `auditwheel`'s reasoning algorithm.

About the wheel in the OP
=========================

The wheel in the OP was built using a Python compiled with `--enable-shared`.
The `manylinux1_x86_64` docker image does not include such a Python (which I believe is excluded by PEP 513), so the first error of `import dd.cudd` is about `libpython3.5m.so.1.0` (an error due to GLIBC_2.14 being required can also happen, but the order appears to be arbitrary).

Installing in the docker image a Python 3.5 compiled with `--enable-shared`
and the wheel, then `import dd.cudd` raises the same error as without `--enable-shared`:

```
ImportError: /lib64/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.14' not found (required by
/opt/_internal/cpython-3.5.3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dd/cudd.cpython-35m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so)
```

PyPI supports hosting of both zip and tar.gz. That is not an issue. That PEP also does not recommend against tar.gz, quite the opposite. From that PEP abstract:

> In addition, this PEP proposes removing support for [...], and any other extension besides .tar.gz and .zip.

Near the end of the document it roughly ends:

> Finally we get down to .tar.gz and .zip. Looking at the pure numbers for these two, we can see that .tar.gz is by far the most uploaded format, with 444,338 total uploaded compared to .zip's 58,774 
> [...]
>
> Instead of trying to standardize on either .tar.gz or .zip, this PEP proposes that we allow either .tar.gz or .zip for sdists.

So, nothing in that PEP recommends against tar.gz format.

For 2. why not just always call build_indexes when instantiating a FilePad rather than counting the number of entries each time and then doing a hard reset depending on the result? 

Note that:
i) The hard reset is kind of a forceful procedure
ii) Creating the index (even when not needed) should be a very lightweight procedure (this used to also be called ensure_index); it will *not* rebuild the index on each call, so it is probably comparable to counting the number of entries in the collection (which you no longer need to do if you just always call build_indexes when creating a FilePad).
iii) If there are multiple threads that are creating FilePad(), e.g., multiple FireWorks running simultaneously, I would be a little less trustworthy of your implementation. There can be a time delay between the count() operation and the delete operations which is not a good thing and in very rare cases can lead to bad behavior. For example, the first process might be deleting the first gridfs collection might take 3 seconds, during which time another process inserts into FilePad (since the length of the documents is now zero, it is all clear to insert), after which the first process deletes the second gridfs collection (including the thing that was just inserted by the the second process)

> A simple question: what is the procedure to backport a PR

I can't talk for the scikit image core team, but at least if you want to use the bot, you mention it on the PR and ask it `backport to <branchname>`
You can also have a label (or a milestone), that have in its `description` `on-merge: backport to <branchname>`, and the bot will try to backport and issue a backport-PR when the principal-PR get merged.

If it can't the bot will give instructions on how to cherry-pick manually (and resolve conflict) as well as add the tag "Still Needs manual backport" in order to make it easy to find the issues needing human attention.

The bot will only reply to committers, or people listed in its [per-repo config file ](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/.meeseeksdev.yml#L2-L4) as being allowed to backport 

You can see (some) statistics of what the bot have done so far [here](https://meeseeksbox.github.io/), more to come.

I have test repos if you want to try how the bot works w/o playing with it here.

Floating point input images have the expectation that they will be on the range [0, 1] (for positive image) or [-1, 1] (for signed images).  There has been talk about relaxing this, but it's not always straightforward as certain algorithms - particularly in contrast adjustment and color conversions - use exponentiation and depend on this assumption.  The output from any algorithm in this package will pass in as the input here without issue. 

We have an "anything in, anything out" policy when it comes to datatypes.  We try to understand what was passed, and hopefully we can work with it, but we don't guarantee it will be returned as the original datatype.  Often we hand it back as a floating point image, so we don't compound rounding error through multiple steps.

The way to try and break this is passing a floating point array - which is a dtype we understand - with values outside this range.  That's what you're doing here.  We could squash the range, but doing so is fraught with a number of assumptions meaning the calculation might be wrong several ways.  Instead, we prefer to throw an informative error and allow the user to correct the problem.

All that said, we probably could remove the `img_as_float` line here without too much trouble.  However, that just means the _next_ function you use will throw this same error at you.  Ideally, get in the habit of following conventions for floating point images.

```text

Usage: objection patchipa [OPTIONS]

  Patch an IPA with the FridaGadget dylib.

Options:
  -s, --source TEXT              The source IPA to patch  [required]

  -k, --skip-cleanup             Do not clean temporary files once finished.
                                 [default: False]
  --help                         Show this message and exit.
```

Because the C++ API was not adapted to support inverse offers and is only offered through the Scheduler HTTP API, there must be a way for the scheduler to adapt to these offers (preferably without changing the signature `resourceOffers` method).

Also, offers and inverse offers are difficult to tell apart without context. I propose that a new method is added to the scheduler interface:

```
def inverseResourceOffers(self, driver, offers):
```

This method should work similarly to `resourceOffers`, but semantics will change framework-to-framework.

sdl2 - UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x98 in position 0: invalid start byte

```

The byte `0x98` is a tilde in Win-1252 (`cp1252`) encoding but not valid `utf-8`. The following uses a fallback which should fix the problem:

```diff
diff --git a/kivy/graphics/shader.pyx b/kivy/graphics/shader.pyx
index 53286893..58b3227c 100644
--- a/kivy/graphics/shader.pyx
+++ b/kivy/graphics/shader.pyx
@@ -630,8 +630,13 @@ cdef class Shader:
         # others drivers doesn't include a \0 (which is great.)
         if length == 0:
             return ""
-        cdef bytes ret = msg[:length]
-        return ret.split(b'\0')[0].decode('utf-8')
+        cdef bytes ret = msg[:length].split(b'\0')[0]
+        try:
+            return ret.decode('utf-8')
+        except UnicodeDecodeError:
+            # fall back to latin1 in case the message isn't valid utf-8
+            return ret.decode('latin1')

     cdef void process_message(self, str ctype, message):
         message = message.strip()
```

This seems like overengineering - this is just a passing comment that a single recipe happens to include, and I don't think there's much general use for recipe information displayed at `__init__` time. Unless this would be widely useful, I think it would be better to just make the BrokenRecipe log a warning instead, or potentially remove it altogether.

@john-westcott-iv Here's some detail about the steps I took, and the problems I ran into.

**EXPORT FROM SOURCE AWX SERVER**
```
awx-cli receive -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://source-awx --credential all > credential.json
awx-cli receive -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://source-awx --project all > project.json
awx-cli receive -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://source-awx --notification_template all > notification_template.json
awx-cli receive -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://source-awx --inventory all > inventory.json
awx-cli receive -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://source-awx --job_template all > job_template.json
awx-cli receive -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://source-awx --workflow all > workflow.json
```

**PRE-IMPORT STEPS ON DESTINATION AWX SERVER**

Assumes we are starting with a clean AWX install.

1. Remove demo Template, Project, Inventory, Credential
2. Update admin password

**IMPORT TO DESTINATION AWX SERVER**

1. Import credentials.

`awx-cli send -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://dest-awx credential.json`

MANUAL STEP: Update passwords/keys on imported credentials to actual values.

2. Import Projects.

MANUAL STEP: Get the PK ID of the appropriate SCM credential for the Projects and update the json.

`awx-cli send -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://dest-awx project.json`

3. Import Notification Templates.

`awx-cli send -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://dest-awx notification_template.json`

MANUAL STEP: Update mail server password.

4. Import Inventory.

MANUAL STEP: In my case, I don't want the hosts/groups imported as I'm using AWS dynamic inventory. So, I remove the `asset_relation { host [] , group [] }` sections.

MANUAL STEP: Update credential PK IDs - they will likely be different that what was exported from the Source server.

`awx-cli send -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://dest-awx inventory.json`

5. Import Job Templates.

`awx-cli send -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://dest-awx job_template.json`

6. Import Workflows.

`awx-cli send -u <user> -p <pass> -h http://dest-awx workflow.json`

**POST-IMPORT**

There doesn't appear to be a way to migrate:

* System settings
* Scheduled jobs
* Permissions

These must all be done manually.

Also, **Job Templates lose their AWS credentials.** Only Machine and Vault credentials appear to be migrated. This is probably the most painful omission, as I have to manually compare and update each imported job template with the source server. However, I've noticed the same thing happens if I copy a template in AWX, so perhaps this is a problem with AWX.

Overall, this is a great start, and goes a long way towards addressing the lack of an upgrade path for AWX. Thank you so much for the work!

@graingert:

> out of interest what do you mean by "without binary wheel support", that's the only feature that pip-accel could provide on top of pip now that pip 6 supports caching?

That's your perspective, it's not mine. From the top of my head:

1. pip-accel implements a [KISS](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KISS_principle) *binary* package cache (that can also be stored in an S3 bucket) without the headaches of setting up a local devpi server and without the headaches of Linux ABI compatibility (think binary wheels hosted on PyPI containing `*.so` files).

2. pip-accel contains support for (automatic) installation of build time dependencies on Debian based Linux distributions (once those build time dependencies have been declared in a configuration file bundled with pip-accel; that file already contains quite a few known build time dependencies).

3. I use pip-accel as a stable API for pip in projects like [py2deb](https://github.com/paylogic/py2deb).

To summarize, if pip provides everything you need then by all means don't bother using pip-accel. If any of the above features are worthwhile however then you know where to find them :-).

Yes, `KUBERNETES_USE_CONFIGMAP` is in Spilo. Somehow in Spilo it is reverted in comparison to Patroni. Because it Patroni I wanted to make a safe choice.

In Patroni kubernetes configs are different:
* `kubernetes.use_endpoints` in yaml or `PATRONI_KUBERNETES_USE_ENDPOINTS` in env
* `kubernetes.namespace` in yaml or `PATRONI_KUBERNETES_NAMESCAPE` in env -- kubernetes namespace where we are running
* `kubernetes.labels` in yaml or `PATRONI_KUBERNETES_LABELS` in env -- These labels will be used to find existing objects (Endpoints|ConfigMaps + Pods) associated with the current cluster. Also Patroni will set them on every object (Endpoint|ConfigMap) it creates/updates.
* `kubernetes.scope_label` in yaml or `PATRONI_KUBERNETES_SCOPE_LABEL` in env -- name of the label containing cluster name. Default value is `cluster-name`
* `kubernetes.role_label` in yaml or `PATRONI_KUBERNETES_ROLE_LABEL` in env -- name of the label containing role (master or replica). Patroni will set this label on the pod it runs in. Default value is `role`
* `kubernetes.pod_ip` in yaml or `PATRONI_KUBERNETES_POD_IP` in env -- ip of the pod we are running in. It's only necessary when `use_endpoints` is enabled, to write this ip into the leader endpoint subsets.
* `kubernetes.ports` in yaml or `PATRONI_KUBERNETES_PORTS` in env -- if the Service object has the name for the port, the same name must appear in the Endpoint object, otherwise service wont work. Example: `{kund: Service, spec: {ports: [{name: postgresql, port: 5432, targetPort: 5432}]}}`. In this case you have to define `kubernetes.ports`: `{[{"name": "postgresql", "port": 5432}]}` and Patroni will use it for updating subsets of a leader Endpoint. This parameter is not used if `use_endpoints` is not set.

As far as I know there are some people/companies running Patroni with Consul in production. I would say it is quite stable.

>  i'm having difficulty trying to get a dockerized set of containers to cluster. I provide PATRONI_CONSUL_HOST to each of my three containers

Have you build your own image or using Spilo? If you are trying to run Spilo with Consul - it will not work. Spilo is only familiar with Etcd, ZooKeeper and Kubernetes API.

There is a one reason why Consul is not supported in Spilo. You have to run a Consul agent in the same container where Patroni and Postgres runs. Upon start this Consul agent have to join some existing Consul cluster. If it fails to join a cluster, it doesn't make much sense even to start Patroni.

> but they each just report that they are master.

Somehow it feels like you are using a Spilo. Since it doesn't support Consul and there is no suitable DCS (etcd, zookeeper) configuration provided from outside, it thinks that container was started locally (testing mode) and decides to start etcd inside the container. Since all etcd are running independently from each other, all nodes are running as masters...

H @jankeirse i, thanks for sharing your tool. You can actually get notifications from etcd of a key change using the watch functionality, i.e. look at https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/etcd.py.

Additionally, did you try to use patroni callbacks, especially on-role-chag?. It requires you to run your script that modifies the configuration from the same host patroni runs at, which might be not what you want, but it has an advantage of being DCS-independent, not tied to etcd or Zookeeper.

Also, instead of asking the etcd, you can query patroni itself (using the REST API), i.e. /master or /slave endpoints in order to find out if the master information stored in the proxy is the same as the one returned by the endpoint (and do some actions depending on the outcome). By talking to Patroni you can avoid dependency on the specific DCS.

As far as functionality, the only thing that we're missing is an easy way to explore what datasets are in the account. I proposed a `list` method in https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/issues/170, but if we go the way of creating a special `Example` CartoContext, then that method would have to be in the CartoContext class as well -- which I think would be a good thing. We could also publish a list of available datasets in the docs and not create a new `list` method, but the docs would need to be kept in sync independently of the cartoframes.carto.com account, which wouldn't be ideal.

We thought of adding more dataset managment functionality in https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/issues/353 based on some user feedback.

So ImageBatch basically abuses marshmallow, because it does not really serialize/deserialize data as originally intended.  What it really does is grab a (validated) string that becomes a directory (presumably full of images) and returns a list of (validated) images discovered there.  While we could make an attempt to take the image batch and re-discover their directory (serialize?) this sounds error prone and not useful.

Is there really a useful semantic definition for serialization in this case?
I've also submitted a patch to DRF core that will fix this issue even if `rest_pandas` is in your `INSTALLED_APPS`.  See https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/pull/3785.

Works well. The only downside is you have to add some decrypt logic in your code along with appropriate decoding.

My helper function ended up looking like this:

```
def decrypt_sec_key_with_kms_key(sec_key):
    # decrypt sec key 
    kms = boto3.client('kms')   
    response = kms.decrypt(CiphertextBlob=base64.b64decode(sec_key))    # decode key before decrypting it
    return response['Plaintext'].decode('utf-8')    # decrypted key comes in bytes literal form, must decode to utf-8
```

This was for python 3.6.5. Hope this helps someone!

@stonebig: ok... so that is taking matters into your own hands.  Cool.  The problem for me is that making `readline` an optional dependency is not feasible, it's too integral of a feature to be able to read from the buffer.  However, your fork does provide for a potential solution going forward.

What do you think of either (1) releasing the fork as a package on pypi, or (2) `dill` using a tarball of your fork on GitHub as a dependency?  Either way, `dill` could depend on that instead of `readline`, at least for the short term.  See: http://python-packaging.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dependencies.html#packages-not-on-pypi. 

I think the better option is (1), because, I believe that (2) will cause some slight issues as noted here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/29170630/how-to-allow-unverified-packages-in-requirements-txt
