@lukeolson13: `pathos` persists it's pools in an internal registry... so that if you create a pool that is "the same" as a previous pool (i.e. same number of nodes, same other configuration parameters), it reuses the old one instead of creating a new pool instance.  Hence, you either have to `restart` an old pool instance that has been "closed", or you have to "delete" the old instance from the registry.

```
>>> square = lambda x:x*x
>>> 
>>> import pathos.pools as pp
>>> pool = pp.ProcessPool()
>>> pool.map(square, range(4))
[0, 1, 4, 9]
>>> pool.close()
>>> pool.join()
>>> 
>>> # we still have the old closed pool instance... so it should fail
>>> pool = pp.ProcessPool()
>>> pool.map(square, range(4))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/mmckerns/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pathos-0.2.2.dev0-py2.7.egg/pathos/multiprocessing.py", line 137, in map
    return _pool.map(star(f), zip(*args)) # chunksize
  File "/Users/mmckerns/lib/python2.7/site-packages/multiprocess-0.70.6.dev0-py2.7-macosx-10.12-x86_64.egg/multiprocess/pool.py", line 250, in map
    assert self._state == RUN
AssertionError
>>> 
>>> # let's restart the pool
>>> pool.restart()
<multiprocess.pool.Pool object at 0x10e185f50>
>>> pool.map(square, range(4))
[0, 1, 4, 9]
>>> # the other option is to remove the old pool instance
>>> pool.close()
>>> pool.join() 
>>> pool.clear()
>>> 
>>> pool = pp.ProcessPool()
>>> pool.map(square, range(4))
[0, 1, 4, 9]
>>> pool.close()
>>> pool.join()
>>> pool.clear()
>>>
```

So if you are seeing a memory spike, then yes, that's probably root cause of the broken pipe.  In that case you definitely want to use `maxtasksperchild` and `_ProcessPool` (or `reset`/`_clear` and `ProcessPool`).  Depending on how long each one of the worker tasks takes (and possibly what you need to serialize), you might try one of the other pools -- so there's `ParallelPool` which has weaker serialization, but the pool is launched through a `subprocessing.Popen` call, so it's a tad slower but the memory handling is cleaner.  There's also a package called `pyina` (it was originally included in `pathos`) that provides a `MPI`-based pool, which also shouldn't have some of the same memory issues, but again, it's typically for larger parallel jobs.  Or there's another route, where you could use `multiprocess` (which is what `pathos` uses under the covers) and go down to the `Process` level of control over your workers.  In the latter case, you can greatly customize how you manage your workers.

If this sufficiently addresses your concerns, please close the issue.

What this could use is an application of the ``@keyedhelper`` decorator I built, which could enable syntax such as this:
```python
content = columns.TextColumn('content', processor=link_to_related_model('fkname'))

This related lookup strategy was neglected because it was it was technically always possible (the example shown in the helper's documentation addresses this) via the ``key`` kwarg.

helpers.link_to_model(key=helpers.attrgetter('fkname'))

Thanks for thinking about this! Much appreciated. It would likely be good to transfer some of this into a doc for easier commenting, but to respond quickly to a few items.

----

> if we think of @console_rule as more of a display driver here, then it's logic could intrinsically handle optimal output interleaving of structured inputs.

Sortof. The issue is that unless an `@console_rule` generates no output until after it has `yielded` for the last time, it's still possible for its output to be interleaved with the running `@rule`s that will be satisfying its `Get` requests. For example, this snippet would result in an interleaving of console and `@rule` output:
```
console.stdout.write('stuff')
more_stuff = yield Get(Thing, OtherThing, x)
console.stdout.write('more stuff')
```

In the curses mode, interleaving is a non-issue. In the "single-output-file" mode (as in naive jenkins without tailing for multiple output files), this results in interleaving. I think I'm convinced that appropriately indented-and-labeled interleavings would be ok, but this is a case that we'll need to be careful of.

----

> would it be a full blown e.g. Task impl, or just a display driver that's accumulating finer grained products and simply displaying those? I'm sort of thinking the latter, but it'd be interesting to sketch this out end to end for the python tasks. for example, we'd discussed the idea of executing py.test and collecting structured output for that in sub-@rules, then aggregating all of that in a @console_rule.

I think my feeling on this is that in order to get something that renders individual test runs as they complete, individual test runs would have to be rendered in the underlying `@rule` output, rather than in the `@console_rule` output. That would mean that in the case of a "test runner"` @console_rule`, it's primary job would be take all `xml` test result output and test logs and place them somewhere in `dist`, and then _maybe_ print out a summary of pass/fail.

The reason I say this is because there is no API currently to "stream" results to an `@console_rule` (or `@rule`). Basically, the result of a `yield` is not an iterator: it's a concrete list. Changing that would be a pretty significant API change I think, and I'm not sure it would be positive.

This is a new realization (for me): will comment more on it in the last paragraph.

----

> or to be able to compute those requests pre-fork somehow, such that we could warm them pre-fork and then re-request post-fork cheaply? It's not immediately clear to me what sort of dynamic product requests one of these sorts of rules would be making that couldn't be computed pre-fork or via a regular, underlying @rule?

This is essentially what I had been thinking of doing on this ticket in the first place, but the big big  challenge in that case is: what does the definition language look like for the queries that run pre-fork?  Basically, it reduces to exactly the problem we had with `SelectProjection`/`SelectDependencies`/etc... it's much more challenging to provide flexibility with a declarative language than it is with the generator-based `@rule`s.

In spite of what I've said above about `@console_rule`s being relatively thin, and `@rule`s doing most of the rendering, I still think it is the case that `@console_rule`s will have complex requirements that involve filtering out target roots (and we never introduced an API to the complex selectors that allowed for filtering), locating tools and subsystems, and etc. So I think that making them generators makes sense.

----

While writing this response, the fact that `@rule`s will need to render things as well has become more clear to me (the test-running example in particular). I think that that means that however we do the virtual console selection, we'll need to provide it to both `@console_rule`s and `@rule`s. The distinctions between the two then currently seem to be:
1. whether filesystem access is available
2. whether more than one is allowed to render at a time
3. the `@interactive_console_rule` bit (although if the answer to 2. is that all `@console_rules` are serialized, then we might not need to distinguish between interactive and non-interactive)

Hi @wsokolow,

samples/sec in training corresponds to the number of sentences per second processed in (sentence- or word-based) batches during training (including the time taken for the update). 
By increasing the batch size, this can be increased trivially (while decreasing updates/sec).
sent/sec in translation corresponds to the number of sentences per second translated. If using batch_size > 1, batches always correspond to sentence-based batches.
If you use word-based batching during training, I would say you cannot really compare two values. 

What we are not reporting at translation time is the average time for a beam search step, i.e. prediction of the next word (for the current sentence or the current sentence batch). This might be useful to have, but I am not sure if we should add this as we are moving towards a beam search implementation that is entirely run by imperative MXNet operations which are evaluated asynchronously. For proper timing of each step, one would have to add `mx.nd.waitall()`'s at the end of an iteration, which might slow down overall decoding speed.

If you're willing to dig into the internals a bit and use the underlying `lxml` interface, you can probably get this done.

Once you have a paragraph, you can get access to any `<w:sdt>` elements inside it using something like this:
```python
paragraph = ... # however you get the paragraph, maybe with `for paragraph in document.paragraphs`
p = paragraph._element
sdts = p.xpath('w:sdt')
for sdt in sdts:
    print('found an sdt element')
```

You can extend the XPath expression to get more detailed items, like `w:sdt/w:sdtPr/w:alias/@w:val'. And when you have an element you can use the lxml API for its _Element object to get its text, attributes, etc. The lxml API is documented here: http://lxml.de/api/index.html, although you'll need to click through a few links to get to the lxml.etree._Element class. This is the direct link: http://lxml.de/api/lxml.etree._Element-class.html, but it's designed in an old-style multi-pane web page so if you want to navigate around the first link is probably better. The lxml tutorial is pretty good too.

You can delete an sdt element like this, once you've found it.
```python
parent = sdt.getparent()
parent.remove(sdt)
```

So one approach might be to loop through the paragraphs, look for ones with an sdt element inside, delete the element, and then add text to the paragraph.

Let us know how you go if you decide to pursue that, would love to see the code you come up with as it will likely be of help to someone else while we're waiting for the API feature to arrive.

Hi @lyw07 - always a pleasure to read your contributions! :) Sorry that I'm so skeptical here, I think the code is the right solution to the wrong problem analysis, so I'm glad you already get the gist of where I'm going :)

> Based on your suggestions, a work-around I'm thinking is to check if the port is occupied before going to daemon mode. One limitation of this work-around is that it only covers one common issue we have. Do you think this will sound better?

Yes, I think we should perform such checks firstly. And they are checks that should all result in user feedback with guidance. A brainstorm:

 * Critical to fix: "Port XXXX is occupied. Close other kolibri processes" (possibly detecting which processes should be closed)
 * Database is corrupted, run `dbrecover` (catching problems with malformed database as described in #3228 )
 * Hard drive is full / inadequate
 * `/path/to/.kolibri` isn't writable

You can see similar principles in Django's check's framework -- unfortunately, we cannot use this, as it's part of Django's stack, and I think we are looking for a solution that can operate before Django's stack is loaded, because `django.setup()` maybe fail in itself, and we want to check these circumstances before even starting it.

I also want to  note that these CLI errors and guides are for people using a terminal. The idea to *only* log critical problems to the terminal aren't actually optimal once we switch to UI control panel apps, where it would be desirable to store these errors in a log file to display in the UI.

Concerning @jamalex 's suggestion:

> We can probably wait in the parent process until the child process either errors or sends a success signal (or perhaps just stay in foreground mode and stream stdout/stderr from the child process until the server successfully starts, then go to background)

 * It's difficult to catch errors from a child process
 * I agree that we should stay in foreground for critical parts, but the port failure is happening when what I would say is really a background task is happening.. we ideally want to yield control to cherrypy, so it can bind+listen and start all its workers... I think things start to get messy here, and there are (as I mentioned in the list above) other things that can go wrong after we yield to the background.

`^(\d{1,9})\.(\d{1,9})(\.|-(\w+))(\d{1,9})(\.\d{1,9})?$`

From [version.go#L104](https://github.com/juju/version/blob/master/version.go#L104)

I think this is the right solution in one sense, because I think what's happening is that the handler gets garbage collected before the event loop, so explicitly closing prior to GC should prevent the error.  Unfortunately, closing it in this helper would cause it to break if called twice, or if anything else tries to use the global event loop afterward.  (It also won't help that damned `test_scp` failure, because the loop that the test uses is managed by (IIRC) the [pytest-asyncio](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pytest-asyncio) plugin and there isn't a place to hook in to close it properly.)

Maybe the best solution would be to add a `close` wrapper to `juju.loop` and document that it should be used (and for the test, investigate the upstream project to get the loop properly closed in that).
Instead, we should just copy to GitHub if relevant for now.

I've taken a look at Kubernetes and Docker Swarm in the context of Deis, and it seems while these may potentially become good products, they are lacking the maturity and feature set of Aurora. 

1) From a scheduling perspective Aurora is the only one that is resource aware at this point in time, which is a major win.

2) Aurora has been battle tested supporting the Twitter platform for the past couple of years. Alternatively Swarm is in alpha, and Kubernetes also seems relatively immature in comparison.

Some digging - docker-registry's [cache options](https://github.com/docker/docker-registry#cache-options) state:

> It's possible to add an LRU cache to access small files. In this case you need to spawn a redis-server configured in LRU mode. The config file "config_sample.yml" shows an example to enable the LRU cache using the config directive cache_lru.

Are we running Redis in LRU mode? From [redis docs](http://redis.io/topics/lru-cache) it sounds like we need to set `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru` in the Redis configuration, which [we're not doing](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/8826c83b28a573334083edc83571488238857924/cache/image/redis.conf#L318-L339).

There may be additional printing commands which are vulnerable to unicode explosions, but this is not trying to solve all of them -- it's purely focused on the fullname issue.

Important note about all of this: there's an argument for explicitly encoding in UTF-8 before writing to disk and decoding upon loading data, but _that's not what configobj is doing_.
It's producing a UTF-8 file, which other tools could choke on. However, given that `configparser` in the SDK doesn't mind this, I'm not sure that we care.

That makes perfect sense. There are a few differences to consider with respect to `fetchmany()`.
* Retrieving an iterator that does just that seems more Pythonic
* `fetchmany()` features dedicated a dedicated `size` parameter that defaults to the `arraysize` attribute. Implementation becomes much simpler without these parameters. Turbodbc already exposes its buffer sizes when creating connections, and these buffer sizes should be used for batched numpy support.

> @carver thoughts on whether this belongs in eth-hash?

Two initial thoughts:

1. It's nice to keep `eth-hash` very simple, so in general converting to bytes does not belong in eth-hash, IMO. For example, converting from a hex string to bytes should stay in eth-utils.
2. `bytes` and `bytearray`  are used nearly interchangeably in various places in the Python standard library. We've typically only supported `bytes` in web3.py for simplicity, but it's a little less consistent with Python standards.

Given point 2, I'm happy to see `eth-hash` support `bytearray`. Similarly, that implies we should also make the conversion utils support `bytearray` in the `primitive` field, like: `to_int`, `to_bytes`, `to_hex`, `to_text`.

Only took a very quick look at the changes and you discussion yet. Thoughts sofar:
- :+1: for `exec`ing to replace the current process.
- :+1: for @mcg1969's idea to reduce the amount of processes involved, i.e., let the first Python process create and `exec` a script that does the activation and `exec`s the requested program.
  BUT: This can be considered an implementation detail and added later on, not necessarily in this PR.
- :+1: to implement this in Python (for flexibility and to avoid more shell-specific shenanigans).

- :-1: to try to use this in shebangs (at least not without additional help like https://github.com/shlevy/long-shebang).
  Shebangs are far to brittle/inflexible since they are not fit (designed) for many modern uses.
  Extremely helpful source: https://www.in-ulm.de/~mascheck/various/shebang/#results
  @mcg1969 encountered the limitations on macOS. Linux has others (127 char limit, argv[2:] not split, i.e., only one argument possible).
- :+1: on making it dumber as the original `conda run`, i.e., not doing the package related stuff.
- :-1: on not making it even dumber :wink:.
- :-1: on the `--` requirement.
  `nargs=argparse.REMAINDER` exists for exactly those use cases.
  Using that, you could just run `conda run -p env-path prog args`.

Regarding that "even dumber" point:
I would expect `conda run -p env-path prog args` to be equivalent to
```sh
(conda activate env-path && exec prog args)
```
meaning, I wouldn't expect `conda run` to check for `prog` to exist in the target environment, but just run the `prog` that is available once `env-path` is activated. That way you could also run foreign application in that evironment, e.g., `conda run which python`, being usually equivalent to `conda run /usr/bin/which python`, which returns the path of the `python` binary that would get called from within that environment. Apart from being more flexible, this would also avoid doing those existence/executable checks manually (instead, e.g., use `exevpe` if running from Python).
If you disagree and like the "`prog` must be in environment" more, that behavior could be made optional with a flag (something like `--env-only`/`--no-env-only`, depending on the default).

We have complex environments and try hard to keep them stable and go MKL all the way. We do not feel anymore like pressing the panic button when CF blas_openblas is installed by some CF package. In fact that rarely happens because we pin all our packages to the channel we want them from. I guess moving channel prioritization to happen before feature minimization could have the same beneficial effect, but if that is too risky to put in conda 4.3, maybe just introducing such working constraints could be a viable alternative?

OpenBLAS and MKL can already be installed in parallel. And in an ideal world, dependent packages linking to one or the other can be mixed at will. If anyone ever wants to mix them, I do not think we need any extra mechanism beyond standard dependency resolution.

Because I have `channel_priority: false` 
```
conda install -c conda-forge scikit-learn
```
won't necessarily install from `conda-forge` - the above will only add `conda-forge` as a channel to search for the latest version and the latest version from whatever channel will be installed. To install from a specific channel I'd have to also add `--override-channels`

> *Would it still be true for you if you replaced the word "channel" with "version"?*

Absolutely! When I choose to install a particular version of a package I don't want that to automatically pin the package to that version. To me installing is separate from pinning whether that's installing a particular version / pinning a package to a particular version or installing from a particular channel / pinning a package to a particular channel. In each case I don't want the install operation to be conflated with the pinning operation.

Python is a good counter-example - if I install a particular python version I probably really do want it pinned at least to the minor version. I can't think of another package though where I'd want automatic pinning.

Thanks for the report.  We'll look into it and see what we need to do to fix it.  We are working on providing a better way of choosing between MKL and openblas (or other blas implementations), and there have been some growing pains.

As a workaround for the time being, I recommend specifying ``numpy-base=1.11`` instead of or in addition to ``numpy=1.11``.

For a bit of back-story, numpy-base is a construct that was added to help remove a circular dependency with mkl_fft and mkl_random.  The numpy package in modern builds is a metapackage depending on numpy-base, mkl_fft and mkl_random.  However, because mkl_random and mkl_fft also depend on numpy, we needed to break that cycle.

> The openblas library appears to have been built only for the Nehalem processor

Thanks we should fix that.

> .. is it the sequential library, a pthreads library, or an OpenMP library?

We have a build matrix dimension for the blas implementation (either MKL or OpenBLAS). Unfortunately that is done by the old 'features' feature. This only allows for two options (feature on or feature off) so we cannot, as things stand, support 3 variants of OpenBLAS (though conda-build 3 - which is not quite ready yet - does have support for non-binary feature).

I am interested in why you want sequential, pthreads and OpenMP though. Can you detail your use-case? I get that sequential can be faster on single core processors, but hardly anyone uses those now, and to my mind, OpenMP vs pthreads is an implementation detail that should be hidden from the user. For packaging, we should pick whichever is the fastest on each platform.

> You can have a look at how it's supposed to be built at the Fedora spec file

That is just one project's version of how it is supposed to be built, they have very different constraints and requirements than those of the Anaconda Distribution (I have not seen a Fedora for macOS or Windows for example). Before you start, understand that Curator v4 is _very_ different from Curator v3. It may not be a good fit for v4.

It's a fair question.  The reason everything is in UTC is because internal to Elasticsearch, everything is in UTC.  The snapshot metadata and index creation date are in epoch format, which is UTC.  Logstash pushing year/month/day index names does rollover at UTC 0:00.  Another reason is that snapshot names have a narrow list of what's acceptable, and adding a timezone information would result in being human readable, but not easily re-parsed back, as only lower-case letters and limited punctuation are allowed in snapshot names.

Curator now does everything in UTC as well.  It would introduce a lot of complexity to handle timezones.  I would have to do it for everything in Curator, and I'm not sure that's something I want to pursue.  This is admittedly in part because it will be a lot of support work trying to answer questions about time zone support, native time zones, people who have things start breaking because they did some weird hack to get around this, etc.  It's been both convenient and predictable to keep things at UTC, as it's not unfamiliar to users of Curator.

I understand it's inconvenient to have to reckon against UTC, but I don't plan on changing this.  If someone wanted to undertake the monumental task of adding timezone support, not just for a narrow use case but across the board, I would probably merge it.  I'm not anticipating that happening, though.

```
try:
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop
    # All code goes here
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    for task in asyncio.Tassk.all_tasks():
        task.cancel()
    loop.run_forever()
    tasks.exception()

finally:
    loop.stop()
```

I agree with @iandees that storing Docker images outside of a Docker "registry" is outside the expected Docker workflow. To clarify, AWS offers a registry service called [EC2 Container Registry (ECR)](https://aws.amazon.com/ecr/). The [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/) is the canonical public registry, although there are alternatives like [Quay](https://quay.io/] from CoreOS. 

That said, I think most people think of [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/) as something like a "github for images." I think it's the first place most people will look and it's also the default registry that Docker Engine itself will look for images. So, for example, if this project were to include a `docker-compose.yml` file and in there it referenced an image like `openaddr/base` then Docker would automatically try downloading an image from `https://hub.docker.com/u/openaddr/base`[1]

the problem is that this change the semantics of the language. The assignment statement does have a value in itself, so it is not obvious what to do. I can see that going into a rabbit hole if you start to deals with value unpacking ellipsis... and so on and so forth. You then get slippery slope and want full macros.

The display part is sufficiently easy, with from IPython.display import display, and it is not that hard to wrap in a function that returns its input:

``` 
from IPython.display import display
def pipe_display(obj):
    display(obj)
    return obj
p = pipe_display
a = p(1)
b = p(2)
c = p(a+b)
```

From the computation point of view, `p` is not there so you can do a lot of things not doable on your proposal :
```
p(3)+p(p(2)*p(7))
```

If `p`is ugly, you can define `__or__` either on `P`, or your objects:
```python
class P:
    
    def __or__(self, other):
        display(other)
        return other
p = P()

class MyObj:
    
    def __init__(self,value):
        self.value = value
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return str(self.value)
    
    def __or__(self, other):
        return other(self)
    
MyObj(1)|p
MyObj(2)|p
p|MyObj('hey')
```

Which is a less clutterd syntax in some sens.

You can execute a notebook from the command line using nbconvert. So if you want to do something like cron... what's wrong with using cron? ;-)

For any kind of scheduled task, cron has several advantages over something cron-like reimplemented in the notebook server:

- Cron is running by default, so your scheduled tasks won't stop when your notebook server is shut down
- There's no need for us to design and manage a new on-disk format to store scheduled tasks across a restart.
- You can run more than one notebook server simultaneously, so we would presumably need some logic to ensure that the same scheduled task is not being run by two servers at once.

See [Python 3 documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextlib.html#contextlib.redirect_stdout), `redirect_stdout` is only in python 3.4 and above. (Tip these info are always at the end of the section).

@UberFarmer, the marshalling filter is only intended for wholesale munging of incoming or outgoing data. Particularly now that the serializable will gain property-like setter functionality, the use cases for the two are completely different, so I don't think there will be too much room for confusion.

That said, you again got me thinking about how to improve this thing. See, the main use case for marshalling transformations is when you have to accommodate data sources/consumers outside of your control. Well, obviously there might be a need for more than one such transformation (one system expects *full_name*, the other expects *first_name* and *last_name*). So instead of having a fixed marshalling logic within a model, it would be better represented as a separate adapter-like object that would be invoked at export time:

```python
instance.to_primitive(adapter=LegacySystemMarshaller)
```
I hope this example also makes it clear why I believe it's useful to be able to perform data manipulation at the very boundary of Schematics. Without it, you would always have to adapt the inner workings of your application according to the specifics of the remote system you are talking to.

Sleeping on the issue, there is a flaw in the plan and I think I'm still stuck needing a `@preflight` equivalent.

Even if the apt layer sets a flag when the system is ready, that only helps for code explicitly using the apt layer. The snap layer, for a real example, does an explicit 'apt install snapd' if snapd is not available (so it works under trusty). It does not use the apt layer, because the snap layer needs to work on snap-only systems. It cannot cannot wait for the apt layer to set a flag, because the charm may not be using the apt layer. But this affects charms using both the apt and snap layers, as they may end up running apt commands before the apt layer has been initialized.

The same issue exists if the apt layer reconfigures the apt subsystem to set reactive states automatically - other layers may attempt to run apt commands before the apt layer has initialized things so they actually work in the deployment environment.

I think this is the same underlying issue that the leadership layer had, except in that case we thought it appropriate to move the leadership layer into core.

And now I think of it, the coordinator layer has the same problem. It implements distributed locking using leadership and the peer relation, so you can easily do things like rolling restarts. If it does not have an opportunity to run code before the queued reactive handlers are run, then handlers may run with expired locks.

I fixed some small issues that we seemed to agree on and I've refactored the data collections to `unit.send` and `unit.receive`. You can view the latest version of the proposal here: https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/blob/2.0/2.0-proposal/RELATIONS.md

I've always thought that `relation-get` and `relation-set` were unfortunate names because each unit has a different view on the relation-data. `relation-set` doesn't change shared relation data, it only changes the relation data that the other unit will see, not the relation data that you see. Conceptually, `send` and `receive` fit the behavior better because they imply that what you send is what the other unit sees.
Of course, this is what it actually looks like in our code, too. Thanks again!

I just wanted to point out that with L1 regularization you will have to generate these features, but they will likely only affect performance (CPU, RAM) only in first few iterations of training; L1 is also a form of feature selection. After a first few iterations coefficients for most of such features are likely to become zero, and in CRFSuite it means they no longer affect computation and no longer take memory. 

You can also use simple heuristics to remove unneeded features, like dropping all features which appear in less than N sequences (e.g. N could be 2 or 5).

So I'd try this first, unless you've already tried it and figured out that L1/ElasticNet is still not enough, or unless you're specifically interested in implementing algorithm from McCallum's paper.

Commenting what the stack currently does:

1. `cli.choose_configurator_plugins()` handles interaction between different command line flags (eg `--apache -i postfix`), and decides whether to call `pick_authenticator`, `pick_installer`, or `pick_configurator` to do final selection (though actually in many cases selection has already occurred, we don't know about issues like misconfiguration of the thing that was selected up here)
2. `pick_installer` / `pick_authenticator` / `pick_configurator`, just a shim layer, marshalls a call to `pick_plugin` adding a question string on the way
3. `pick_plugin` either confirms the selected plugin is prepared/available, **or** makes a list of plugins that are interface-verified, prepared & available, and calls `choose_plugin` to choose between them if there are several.
4. `choose_plugin` constructs the data structures for a menu, calls `iDisplay.menu()`, and processes the result.

I found a serious bug in your code that allows "SQL Injection" vulnerability.

     >>> Opportunity.objects.filter(name__range=("\\", 'z'))
    SalesforceError: MALFORMED_QUERY
    >= '\' AND Opportunity.Name <= 'z' LIMIT 21

Hi @aseemw, thanks for your reply. In keras, before the blstm layer is a lambda reshape layer, whose input shape is [None,32,32,512]. Since the batch size is 1, the input shape of lambda_1 layer can be specified as [1,32,32,512]. The reshape function change the shape from [1,32,32,512] to [32,32,512] for blstm to use. 
```
def reshape(x):
    import tensorflow as tf 
    b = tf.shape(x)
    x = tf.reshape(x,[b[0]*b[1],b[2],b[3]])
    return x

def reshape2(x):
    import tensorflow as tf 
    x1,x2 = x
    b = tf.shape(x2)
    x = tf.reshape(x1,[b[0],b[1],b[2],256])
    return x 

x1 = Lambda(reshape,output_shape=(32,512),name='lambda_1')(x) 
x2 = Bidirectional(LSTM(128,return_sequences=True), name='blstm')(x1)
x3 = Lambda(reshape2,output_shape=(None,None,256),name='lambda_2')([x2,x])
```
While in coreml, all shapes are described in 5-D array. Thus the input shape of lambda_1 layer is [1,1,512,32,32] and the output shape of it remains the same. So in the implementation swift class of the custom layer lambda_1, I do not changed the outputshape. I think I have provided the right dimension at runtime in coreml.
```
func outputShapes(forInputShapes inputShapes: [[NSNumber]]) throws -> [[NSNumber]] {
        // inputShapes: [[1, 1, 512, 32, 32]]
        // outputShape: [[1, 1 ,512, 32,  32]]
        let outputShape = inputShapes
        return outputShape
    }
```

I'll just note that most of the interesting floating-point verification work actually involves showing that certain operations will produce exact results. The typical approach is to take two floats, convert them to reals, do the operation with infinite precision, and turn the result back to a float using the rounding mode. Then, one shows that this result is precisely the same as just doing the corresponding operation directly on the floats without moving to reals and back. (This is the required behavior in the IEEE754 sense.) 

I'd even say that this is the raison d'etre for why we want an automated floating-point logic in the first place.

Of course, none of this is criticism for Z3 or SMT. It's just that such problems are hard and more research is needed. But without proper real/float conversion support in solvers, the automation is inherently limited in its industrial use.

We haven't exposed compile-time slicing. Indeed the size of the library / executable has been steadily growing and will keep growing as features are added. The plugin architecture is dynamic and for theory solvers or tactics that are used in some cases. If you would want to slice away tactics or solvers it would have to be in a specialized build. Not sure how strongly the linker slice away dead code. If it does a good job, then perceivably one can slice tactics away to creating compile time variants of the ADD_TACTIC macro. Other components are tougher to prune. For example, nlsat is a standalone module that is used 2-3 places as part of tactics (and any time also theory solvers). the "sat" module is used for bit-vector intensive problems. It's likely you need this. The "lp" module solves LRA at this point over theory_lra. etc.

I think it's a safe assumption - doesn't it say somewhere in the docs that layers are meant to operate on and produce Theano expressions? Plus, usually in machine leaning people represent unusual data types as tensors anyways (e.g. strings as one-hot vectors).

> Could you please explain how to exactly initialize the sub kernels?

You basically initialize weights for smaller convolution kernels (scaled down by the stride, this is what they refer to as subkernels), and then upscale the kernels to their intended size by nearest neighbor interpolation (= by replicating the pixel values). This ensures the dilated convolution initially behaves like a small convolution followed by nearest neighbor upscaling, and then refines the weights from there.

> I guess we should add a test that checks if they are equal then, or something like that?

That's not easily possible. One of the version strings lives in `setup.py` (which cannot import `__init__.py` because that version of Lasagne isn't installed yet), and the other one lives in `__init__.py` (which cannot import `setup.py` because that's not installed along with Lasagne). We could move the version string into a data file that's read with `open()` and installed along with Lasagne, or we could have `setup.py` parse the `__init__.py` file instead of importing it (I think I've seen this technique being used somewhere, it would just have to find the `__version__ = ` assignment).

> Seems like something that could be easily forgotting when creating a new release, because it happens so rarely.

There are only two places. It would be very easy to put a comment at the first place to update the second place and vice versa!

> I'm just going to put in another plug here for versioneer.

I think using versioneer just to keep the two strings synced would be overkill, but it would give us version strings including the git commit.

I'm closing this as I found a tradeoff / good solution for my case:

```python
project.hook(addr=0x400e5b, hook=indirect_blx_hook)

    l.info("Custom ARM32 Hook executed")

    if state.thumb:
        state.regs.pc = state.regs.pc + 2
        state.regs.r14 = state.regs.pc
    else:
        state.regs.pc = state.regs.pc + 4
        state.regs.r14 = state.regs.pc
```

This circumvents the length specification and is more generic, or in other words - the pc incrementation is left in the responsibility of the specific hook, which I like way more.

The only remaining question is if this would've been possible with already existing Sim-Procedures (e.g. Caller) which I did not get to work properly.

All you need is to get a `SimIRSB` instance, and then access the `irsb` property of it.

Two ways:
- If you already have a `CFGNode`, and you have input states for it (`keep_input_states=True` was specified when building the `CFG`), then `irsb = cfg._get_irsb(cfg_node).irsb` gives you a reference to the basic block (`IRSB` is how VEX names it).
- Getting the `IRSB` (not the `SimIRSB`, which is essentially `IRSB` plus an initial state) does not necessarily mean you must have the initial state. So the following lines are easier:
```
block = proj.factory.block(<address>)
irsb = block.vex
irsb.pp() # For pretty printing
```
where `proj` is a reference to your angr project.

Moreover, the `Block` object has more power than just yielding an `IRSB`. Simply try `block.capstone.pp()`. @rhelmot designed the `Block` interface, which I really like :-)

Okay so I dug in a bit more. It needs some explanation.

First off let me say that python-bugzilla is an old API and has lots of historical baggage so some things can't be changed quickly without risk of breaking other users.

The getcomponents() API behind the scenes actually doesn't use Product.get, it uses Bug.legal_values({"product_id": id, 'values': 'components'}. This API only takes a product_id which is an integer. So when a product string name is passed in, we need to do a Product.get call to fetch all product info to map name->id.

legal_values is deprecated upstream so it's unlikely it will ever be expanded to take a product name. API docs say that legal_values should be replaced with Bug.fields however I can't see any way to actually do the equivalent of ask for all component names for a particular product. Boo

This same functionality can be implemented like you do with Product.get, however on bugzilla.redhat.com for Products like Fedora where there are 1000s of components, even a minimal Product.get call asking for only include_fields=[components.name] is 10x slower than legal_values. So changing the backend impl is going to possibly break things for anyone listing Fedora component names or similar

I updated the code to be a bit more efficient with the implicit Product.get call here, only asking for include_fields=["name", "id"] which should make it a bit faster. But in the short term that's the best we can do. Long term I will likely have to implement this with Product.get or hopefully upstream absorbs rhbz's custom Component.get, but I won't be exposing that in python-bugzilla API since we've been bitten by exposing non-upstream APIs

There is an aspect missing here. The sparkmagic itself should not do any
pretty printing at all. It should simply return data frames. The pretty
printing can then be done in two ways:

1) Passing those data frames to Python functions which do the pretty
printing.
2) Using the IPython display system to register those functions as the
default display representations for data frames.

The advantage of 1) is that you can offer multiple rich representations of
data frames for users and let them pick which is used. The downside of 1)
is that it isn't automatic.

The advantage of 2) is that it is automatic and will make *all* data frames
appear nice. The downside is that it is automatic so users can only get
that representation automatically.

My advice is this:

* Only return data frames.
* Build a set of function which take a data frame and do *one* particular
rich representation (table or vis)
* Then write functions which optionally enable one of those display
function as the automatic display function for data frames.

It is really important to keep the scope of the sparkmagic narrow - it
shouldn't know anything about visualization, tables or other pretty
printing.

Does this make sense?


On Tue, Dec 8, 2015 at 4:44 PM, msftristew <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> I am currently investigating solutions to this issue. Here is the problem
> we're facing:
>
>    1.
>
>    If we want to enable pretty-printing, the magics necessarily need to
>    have a side effect. So Livy would give us a dataframe, and the spark magic
>    would return None. Returning None silences the kernel so nothing else
>    would be printed to the screen.
>    2.
>
>    We can't return the result of the magic because if we did, then that
>    would frequently cause results for an invocation to be printed twice: once
>    (pretty-printed) by the magics, and once (improperly formatted) by the
>    kernel.
>    3.
>
>    However, this prevents users from being able to do something like x =
>    %sql SELECT * FROM..., or from doing %sql SELECT * FROM ... and using
>    the Out[] array to get the dataframe from an invocation. This limits
>    the usefulness of the magics.
>
> If we all agree this functionality is necessary, then we have a few
> options.
>
>    1.
>
>    Give different commands different names. So we would have one command
>    for running sql queries and pretty-printing the results, and one other
>    command for running sql queries and getting the results of the query
>    as a dataframe. Alejandro and I think this is a pretty messy solution.
>    2.
>
>    Add an option to silence and return the output of the magic. So for
>    example one could write %sql SELECT * FROM ... -r, which would have no
>    output and simply return the dataframe. What's unfortunate here is that you
>    then can't pretty-print the results and also save the results as a
>    dataframe, since silencing and returning the output are captured in the
>    same option.
>    3.
>
>    Similarly to option 2, add two options: -r, which returns the
>    dataframe instead of None, and -q, which silences the pretty-printer.
>    This has all the power that one would need from the system, but now
>    pretty-printing the output while also capturing the results as a dataframe
>    (which might be a common pattern for some users) becomes pretty verbose: %sql
>    SELECT * FROM ... -r -q.
>
> What do you think? Do we want the more verbose but more powerful options
> for the magics?

yes, but why make it awkward and force the developer to make that conversion? Unless there is some real benefit to having it be a tuple at the end of the day.

>> Similarly, adding 2 tuples to a new composite tuple with "+" works just fine, right?

Yes, you could also keep redefining your original tuple. e.g.,

```
x = ("citation1",)
if something:

return x

I've done a bit more thinking and research here and this might be a bit more complicated than we initially thought.  My original assumption was that lot of the performance benefit of the glob approach had to do with reducing back-and-forth across the python/C++ boundary.  But this is belied by the fact that the existing gfile.Walk()-based implementation actually is fine on local disk - it executes quickly even for very large logdirs.

My new interpretation is that the real speedup comes from reducing the number of round trips from local C++ code to a remote filesystem and back.  Globbing achieves a huge reduction in round trips when the remote file system implements TF's FileSystem.GetMatchingPaths() in the equivalent of a single possibly-streaming RPC, which *is* true of CNS (an internal-only filesystem) and happens to also be essentially true for GCS ([link to code](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/v1.7.0/tensorflow/core/platform/cloud/gcs_file_system.cc#L1076)).  However, this isn't generally true for other remote TF FileSystem implementations or even for other internal Google filesystems.  In those cases, each level of the glob actually does a full gfile.Walk() - `check if dir; if so, list it; recurse on results` (it should be a depth-bounded walk, but the FileSystem default implementation doesn't actually do any such bounding...).  So for those remote FileSystems we're now doing D+1 times as much work and sending D+1 times as many RPCs as the existing approach.

Given that issue, I think we should probably make this behavior conditioned on the underlying filesystem implementation, and then enable it only when we know the filesystem is well-suited to it.  The "correct" way to do that would arguably be adding another C++-level TF FileSystem method for this, but for now we could also just add a heuristic within our codebase that checks the known path prefixes.

Note that if this interpretation of the speedup is correct, then writing our own C++ code won't necessarily add much additional speedup, because there the real limitation is what RPCs the remote filesystem exposes.  With GCS, if we had access to the underlying RPC interface we could implement recursive listing in a single (up to pagination) RPC, so it could be worthwhile.  But with CNS, the C++ code doesn't have access to any RPC that's more efficient for our purposes here than the one used by glob, and unlike GCS that one is limited to returning one directory level at a time, so there's not much overhead we could remove without literally writing new RPCs for CNS.

@turon: Wi-Fi uses the model I implemented here: *promiscuous* mode means network-only, whereas *monitor* is the big fat firehose. This convention is [well established](http://security.stackexchange.com/questions/36997/what-is-the-difference-between-promiscuous-and-monitor-mode-in-wireless-networks), and I think it has utility to wireless network debugging in general—which is why I have adopted that convention here.

In cases where the underlying hardware doesn't support the concept of filtering on only the PANID versus no filtering at all, changing the mode to `PHY_MODE_PROMISCUOUS` would result in `PHY_MODE_MONITOR` being set instead. (Or, if we wanted to, we could implement that filtering in software)

```
import tensorflow as tf
import tflearn as tfl

DIM = {"one_hot" : 20, "physicochemical" : 4}

##========

def l2_angle_distance(predict, actual):
    """ 
    Customized loss function for backbone angle calculations in protein
    folding.  Cosine distances are calculated, and then squared, to
    give the loss function quadratic (L2) behavior.  NaN values are masked.
    """
    with tf.name_scope("L2AngleDistance"):
        # Calculate a scaling factor: the number of FINITE values in 
        # actual, must be typecast to a tf.float32.
        count = actual[...,0,0]
        scale = tf.to_float(tf.count_nonzero(tf.is_finite(count)))
        # Mask actual: change all NaN in actual to whatever was predicted.
        # Stops NaN from propagating into the loss calculation.  This will
        # instead calculate cosine distances of zero for any rows where 
        # actual was NaN.
        actual = tf.where(tf.is_nan(actual), predict, actual)
        # Supply the -1 argument for axis (that TFLearn can't pass in)
        losses = tf.losses.cosine_distance(predict, actual, -1, 
                 reduction=tf.losses.Reduction.NONE)
        # Square the losses, then sum, to get L2 scalar loss.
        # Finally, divide the loss result by the scaling factor.
        return tf.reduce_sum(losses * losses) / scale

##========

class MyDNNSubclass(tfl.DNN):

    def __init__(self, window, topology, encoding="one_hot"):
        self.window = window
        self.topology = topology
        self._keep_prob = 2/3  # Make this a hyperparameter?
        if encoding not in ("one_hot", "physicochemical"):
            raise ValueError("Encoding must be 'one_hot' or 'physicochemical'.")
        self.encoding = encoding
        if window == "whole_protein":
            net = self._convolutional_net(topology, DIM[encoding])
        else:
            if (window < 3) or (not window % 2):
                raise ValueError("Window must be 'whole_protein', or an odd integer, at least 3.")
            net = self._dense_net(window, topology, DIM[encoding])
        net = tfl.regression(net, optimizer="adam", learning_rate=0.001, \
                             loss=l2_angle_distance)
        super().__init__(net) # Checkpoints not working?  Verbosity not working?
    
    def _convolutional_net(self, topology, width):
        # Input placeholder
        net = tfl.input_data(shape=[None, None, width])
        # Need to keep two dimensions of input shape, see below
        sh = tf.shape(net)
        # Add all trainable layers to the graph, except the last.  For
        # now, every layer convolves with a window of 3.
        for size in topology:
            net = tfl.conv_1d(net, size, 3)
            net = tfl.dropout(net, keep_prob=self._keep_prob)
        # The last trainable layer always has a fixed size of six outputs.
        net = tfl.conv_1d(net, 6, 1)
        # Output layers, reshape and normalize, not trainable.
        net = tfl.reshape(net, [sh[0], sh[1], 3, 2])
        net = tf.nn.l2_normalize(net, dim=3)
        return net
    
    def _dense_net(self, window, topology, width):
        # Input placeholder
        net = tfl.input_data(shape=[None, window*width])
        # Add all trainable layers to the graph, except the last.
        for size in topology:
            net = tfl.fully_connected(net, size)
            net = tfl.dropout(net, keep_prob=self._keep_prob)
        # The last trainable layer always has a fixed size of six outputs.
        net = tfl.fully_connected(net, 6)
        # Output layers, reshape and normalize, not trainable.
        net = tfl.reshape(net, [-1,3,2])
        net = tf.nn.l2_normalize(net, dim=2)
        return net
    
    def fit(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.restarts = 0
        tf.reset_default_graph()  # https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/issues/408
        cb = MonitorCallback()
        while True:
            try:
                super().fit(*args, **kwargs, callbacks=cb)
            except NonFiniteResult:
                # (See Note 1)
                self.restarts += 1
                print("> NON FINITE result, restart #{} <\n".format(self.restarts))
                continue
            except StopIteration:
                pass
            break
        self.epoch = cb.epoch
        self.min_val_loss = cb.min_val_loss
    
##========

class NonFiniteResult(Exception):
    pass

##========

class MonitorCallback(tfl.callbacks.Callback):
    
    # https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/helpers/trainer.py
    # See lines 1059 - 1079.
    def __init__(self, long=8, short=4):
        self.val_loss = np.zeros((0,))
        self.lengths = -long, -short
        self.epoch = 0
    
    def on_epoch_end(self, training_state):
        self.epoch += 1
        if not np.isfinite(training_state.global_loss):
            raise NonFiniteResult
        self.val_loss = np.append(self.val_loss, training_state.val_loss)
        long_mean, short_mean = [self.val_loss[x:].mean() for x in self.lengths]
        if long_mean / short_mean < 0.999:
            self.finish(training_state)
            raise StopIteration
    
    def on_train_end(self, training_state):
        self.finish(training_state)
    
    def finish(self, training_state):
        self.min_val_loss = self.val_loss.min()

```

I just realized that there is another way to handle multiple outputs in `numpy.vectorize()`:

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> def return_multiple_values(x):
...     return x, x + x, x * x
... 
>>> v = np.vectorize(return_multiple_values)
>>> v(1)
(array(1), array(2), array(1))
>>> v([2, 3])
(array([2, 3]), array([4, 6]), array([4, 9]))
```

This could probably also be implemented in pybind11, using `std::tuple`, but for my use case this wouldn't be applicable.

If your `std::list` was actually a `std::vector` then I think `pybind11/stl_bind.h`'s `bind_vector` would be the answer.  Without binding the container, all you get (via `stl.h`) is copying of the returned list contents into a native Python list, which doesn't seem like what you want.

The problem is that we don't actually *have* a `bind_list` in `stl_bind.h`, but I think that that (hypothetical) feature is pretty much exactly what you're looking for.  The good news is that it would probably be fairly easy to implement using some of the existing `bind_vector` code, but with SFINAE checks for the various definitions that rely on `operator[]` (like `__getitem__`/`__setitem__`/`__delitem__`).  It might also need some tweaks to allow for non-copyable element types (e.g. your `B` elements in the example), and to only call `reserve()` if the container has such a method.

(It might also make sense to mimic (some of) the [`deque`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html#collections.deque) interface since a `std::list` is far closer to a Python `deque` than a Python `list`.)

And in case it wasn't obvious, I'm hinting that a PR implementing this would be very welcome. :)

Guys, do not mix ```yum``` installed packages via ```pip``` ones.  The proper fix involves removing pip packages and install everything from ```yum```. I have outlined it [here](https://www.getpagespeed.com/troubleshooting/fix-importerror-pyopenssl-module-missing-required-functionality-try-upgrading-to-v0-14-or-newer).

You do not need newer pyOpenSSL on CentOS 7 to run certbot! Not to say relying on third packages will not lead you to anything good, eventually :)

```bash
pip uninstall requests
yum reinstall python-requests

pip uninstall six
yum reinstall python-six

pip uninstall urllib3
yum reinstall python-urllib3
```

An additional note on existing `IDisplay` methods:

 * By convention, inputs of all types seem to support a cancel operation (by special handling of "c"). However, I can't find anywhere that actually does something _interesting_ with cancellation (in the end, it seems like an exception is eventually raised). It's not clear what value that functionality adds (over, say, just having them do their system's equivalent of `ctrl+c`), and the pattern results in conditional logic sprinkled after every user interaction. If there are cases I missed where cancellation is important, it seems like a very uncommon case and therefore better handled outside of the common codepath (e.g., with a wrapper or decorator of some sort).

This test case appears to be a test of [F.6.2 Out-of-range parameters], which (among other) states:

```
Any nonzero value for either of the flags fA or fS is taken to mean the value 1.
```

And the particular test case contains:

```xml
    <!-- out of range large-arc-flag value -->
    <path d="M280,120 h25 a25,25 0 1,0 -25,25 z" fill="lime" stroke="lime"/>
    <path d="M280,120 h25 a25,25 0 6 0 -25,25 z" fill="red"/>
```

In other words, we are expected to accept the single digit "6" as a "1".  As far as I understand it, this implies that the following is *also* valid:

```xml
    <path d="M280,120 h25 a25,25 0 60 -25,25 z" fill="red"/>
```

And should be parsed identically.


[F.6.2 Out-of-range parameters]: https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG11/single-page.html#implnote-ArcOutOfRangeParameters

Well, obviously people could create CSS that overrides properties of gradients as well, but considering we do not account for any style attribute on gradients they are unfortunately "doomed" either way...

Scour currently only supports inline CSS, so using it in a different way is likely to cause undefined/broken behavior.

I think it's safe to remove duplicate gradients for now unconditionally (I can only try to make an educated guess myself...) and is probably what the majority of users would expect.

Seeing that there are more attributes than just "order" which allow a "<number-optional-number>" value I'd say it would be more sensible to either
a) disable scouring for them for now
b) implement a proper solution

A proper solution would probably consist of two code changes:
- a new step in "scourString()"  
  (we'd need to check if "first number == second number" in which case the second number could be removed)
- a change in "removeDefaultAttributeValue()" to account for lists of numbers
   One possible implementation could be to pass the DefaultAttribute's value as a list in this case (i.e. `[3]`), so we can add a new case for "lists of numbers" that need to be split first (we could likely bail from this function as soon as splitting results in more than a single token)

That said I have no idea how often any of these attributes are used (and if they are used how often they are used as a list) - probably not very often, otherwise we'd have seen reports already.
If they are as uncommon as I assume checking them as strings might just be be fine after all. but at the very least the workaround should be implemented for all affected attributes in this case)

Basically, after the tasks are completed (print `completed`), the task engine has its own timing to check the status of the tasks, which basically calls a `sos status` command (a separate process) at every 2 seconds (on local host) to check the status of tasks. This is the only way to check the status of remote tasks but we could optimize it for local tasks.

`%toc` is good enough when editing a notebook so I think inserting a TOC in the notebook is primarily used to add a TOC to the exported HTML report (through `%sossave` magic). However, automatic generation of a TOC is a common practice for many HTML templates (e.g. [Rstudio report](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/html_document_format.html)) so there is less a need to insert it at the notebook level. I am therefore thinking that adding TOCs to our templates (e.g. [sos-report template](https://github.com/vatlab/sos-notebook/blob/master/src/sos_notebook/sos-report.tpl)) is more flexible (because we can have many templates in different styles) and therefore a better choice.

That said, I am not a HTML/JS expert and it is challenging for me to write customized Jupyter templates to produce reports as nice as what RStudio/Knitr is generating. Perhaps I should dissect a knitr report and borrow its TOC code even CSS. :smile:

*Apologies for the developer evangelism/long-post below, looking for other projects that may find work I've done recently useful*

---

An alternative to adding custom Java code would be to use [Skein](https://jcrist.github.io/skein/), a tool/library I wrote to make deploying applications on YARN easier. Skein is what [Dask](http://dask.pydata.org/) uses for its YARN deployments. It uses a declarative specification (either written as a yaml file, or using the python api), and handles all the YARN stuff without the user ever touching java.

I looked at writing a proof-of-concept for deploying Ray on YARN using Skein, but ran into a few issues (more on that later). So the following is pseudo-code, but hopefully the logic is still clear:

```yaml
name: ray

services:
    ray-head:
        instances: 1
        resources:
            # The resources for the head node.
            vcores: 2
            memory: 2048
        files:
            # A packaged python environment using `conda-pack`. Note that Skein
            # doesn't require any specific way of distributing files, but this
            # is a good one for python projects.
            environment: environment.tar.gz
        commands:
            # Activate the packaged conda environment
            - source environment/bin/activate
            # This command would start all the processes needed on the ray head
            # node, and register any addresses needed by the workers with the
            # Skein key-value store. It should then block until killed with sigterm
            # (at which point all started processes should also die).
            - start-ray-head

    ray-worker:
        # The number of instances to start initially. This can be scaled
        # dynamically later.
        instances: 4
        resources:
            # The resources for the worker node
            vcores: 2
            memory: 2048
        files:
            environment: environment.tar.gz
        depends:
            # Don't start any worker nodes until the head node is started
            - ray-head
        commands:
            # Activate the packaged conda environment
            - source environment/bin/activate
            # This command would start all the processes needed on a ray worker
            # node, getting any addresses it needs for e.g. the head node from
            # the skein key-value store. It should then block until killed with
            # sigterm (at which point all started processes should also die).
            - start-ray-worker
```

A few key points:

- The above specification describes two services: `ray-head` and `ray-worker`. Both are composed of what files are needed to run them, and what commands are needed to start each service. The full specification format is [here](https://jcrist.github.io/skein/specification.html)

- In the above pseudocode, [conda-pack](https://conda.github.io/conda-pack/) is used to distribute a python environment containing Ray, its dependencies, and any other libraries the user might want on each node. The tarball works transparently with YARN's resource localization mechanism, and allows the user to distribute the necessary resources without having IT deploy stuff manually. Note that skein is agnostic to this, so files could already be available locally, or distributed using any other mechanism. For more information see https://jcrist.github.io/skein/distributing-files.html.

- The commands to start each service are pseudocode (I couldn't figure out how to write them, but someone familiar with Ray could easily). `start-ray-head` needs to start all processes needed for the ray head node and register any *runtime* configuration details in the [skein key-value store](https://jcrist.github.io/skein/key-value-store.html). This would be things like the redis address. `start-ray-worker` can then get this address from the key-value store and use it as needed. Both commands then need to block indefinitely until shutdown, and if killed with `SIGTERM` shutdown all started processes.

This is the main problem I ran into when trying to write a proof-of-concept. The `ray start --block` command doesn't kill subprocesses when killed. When YARN stops a container it sends `SIGTERM` (and later `SIGKILL` if that doesn't work). Killing the top-level process should result in cleanup of all child processes, which currently doesn't seem to be the case (`kill -15 $PID_OF_RAY_START_BLOCK` doesn't cleanup child processes).

Given the above specification, starting, scaling, and stopping the cluster using the [`skein` cli](https://jcrist.github.io/skein/cli.html) is as simple as:

```console
# Start the application, and record the application id
$ appid=`skein application start spec.yaml`

# Scale up to 10 workers
$ skein container scale $appid --service ray-worker --number 10

# Shutdown the applicaiton
$ skein application shutdown $appid
```

Alternatively, this is all doable using the [native python api](https://jcrist.github.io/skein/api.html), which is what [`dask-yarn`](https://github.com/dask/dask-yarn) uses.

---

Skein is [tested](https://github.com/jcrist/hadoop-test-cluster) on clusters with and without kerberos enabled (as well as a slew of other configurations), eliminating a class of bugs you'd need to test for if you wrote your own application master. It also significantly shortens the amount of code you need to support yarn (`dask-yarn` is ~500 lines, including docstrings).

There are arguments against using it (it's new-ish, an added abstraction layer, etc...), but I truly believe for non-jvm projects it's at least a good option to think about when looking at YARN support.

I'd be happy to help someone work on adding YARN support to Ray using Skein, but I don't currently have bandwidth to work on this personally. Just thought I'd let you know another option exists.

When `PlayerOutput._create_arguments` calls:
```python
title = subprocess.list2cmdline([self.format_args["title"]])
```
`list2cmdline` places double quotes around the returned title. *However*, not only is it not quoting the whole title, in the failing examples there are also extra double quotes, so most of the title gets unquoted. For example, if you run it with:
```streamlink -p mpv -a "--title=\"TEST {title}\" {filename}" https://www.twitch.tv/esl_sc2```
the call arguments get mangled to:
```mpv --title="TEST "esl_sc2 - LIVE: GuMiho vs. jjakji [TvT] - Bracket 3 - IEM Katowice 2017 - Day 2" "<video url>"```

My understanding is the main task is to supply some array with a "tracer number" dimension to the code. We can create a `TracerPacker` object (maybe by another name) which does what we need and can be used by components if they desire. It would need to have the following methods:

- `register_tracers(dims, unit_dict, input_properties)` where `dims` is a dim specification the tracers will be fit to, such as `['*', 'mid_levels']`, `unit_dict` is a mapping from quantity names to dimensions, and `input_properties` is the property dictionary on a component to which it will insert properties for these tracers. This method would also internally store the tracer names, to be used by `pack_tracers` and `unpack_tracers` below. This would be called within a component's `__init__`.
- `pack_tracers(raw_state)` which takes in the state of numpy arrays (such as given to `array_call`) and returns an array of dimension `['tracer_number'] + tracer_dims` containing the tracers.
- `unpack_tracers(tracer_array)` which takes a tracer array of the form returned by `pack_tracers` and returns a dictionary of numpy arrays (one for each tracer)

When multiple components are handling tracers, all the user has to do is pass in the same tracer unit dictionary to multiple components. `dims` would be determined by the component (a dynamical core for instance would specify horizontal dimensions, while a convective component would not).

This could potentially be used for other dynamic components, like components that should relax a set of desired quantities towards zero or smooth them in the vertical or ensure they're conserved from timestep to timestep. For this reason, perhaps we should name it `QuantityPacker` or something similar.

Right, what `nursery.spawn` does is immediately create a task, graft it into the task tree at the given nursery, and then mark the task runnable. And the next time the scheduler runs – which might be when you reach the end of the nursery's `async with`, but it could be earlier, too, if you execute any checkpoints inside the `async with` body – then it cycles through all the runnable tasks in some random order. So it's much closer to the thread spawn model than the magic await.

It looks like #136 will lead to some simplification in the nursery API, which may also make this a little easier to explain, because the body of the `async with` will stop being magical and become effectively just another task running inside the nursery.

In any case, your problem – wanting to run initialization code in a deterministic order – is an important one; it comes up all over the place. (Another common example: in a test suite, if you start a server and then start a client that connects to that server, you want to know that the server is fully started and ready to accept connections *before* you start the client.)

Here's one possible pattern: define a `start` function that takes a nursery, and it makes two guarantees: (a) it will spawn the appropriate thing into the nursery, and (b) it will not return until the thing is "ready", whatever that means. (For a server, it would mean: listening socket is bound and ready to accept connections.) In particular, point (b) is what gives you the ordering guarantees that raw `spawn` is lacking.

So in your case, if I'm understanding your pseudo-code right, this might look something like:

```python
class Child:
    def start(self, nursery):  # or 'async def', if the initialization might be async
        self.initialize_this_object()
        nursery.spawn(self._main)

    async def _main(self):
        async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
            for child in self.children:
                child.start(nursery)

async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
    root.start(nursery)
```

So first, there is a way to rewrite our websocket fixture above so that it works. This is ugly, but at least it's a workaround while we're figuring out better options:

```python
async def hold_websocket_in_task(*, task_status=trio.TASK_STATUS_IGNORED):
    async with open_websocket(...) as websocket:
        task_status.started(websocket)
        # Do need to be prepared for an exception here... in this example it's fine, but in others it 
        # might take some care
        await trio.sleep(float("inf"))

@pytest.fixture
async def websocket_client(test_nursery):
    return await test_nursery.start(hold_websocket_in_task)
```

But surely we can do better than that though.

One temptation is to say fine, this wouldn't be a problem if pytest fixtures actually worked like context managers, so maybe trio fixtures *should* act like context managers. (Well... this wouldn't let us parallelize fixture setup/teardown, but it would at least solve the sequential case.) However, this would break a *lot* of existing fixture patterns, e.g. this example from the docs:

```python
   @pytest.fixture
   async def rolback_db_connection():
       # Setup code
       connection = await some_async_db_library.connect(...)
       await connection.execute("START TRANSACTION")

       # The value of this fixture
       yield connection

       # Teardown code, executed after the test is done
       await connection.execute("ROLLBACK")
```

Also, the way our fixture handling works, you actually need to know a pretty complex set of rules before you can tell which fixtures are Trio fixtures, and which are pytest fixtures. Especially the "if it depends on a trio fixture, it's a trio fixture" rule would be pretty confusing if it also totally changed the semantics of `yield` in this subtle-yet-fundamental way. If we were to go this way, I think we'd really have to force people to use `@trio_fixture` instead of automagically supporting `@pytest.fixture`, because the magic is too, well, magic. (I guess this wouldn't be the end of the world anyway?)

But really the problem isn't exceptions in general... and we don't particularly want to allow fixtures to catch and transform errors from tests; that could get super confusing. The problem is specifically `Cancelled` exceptions. Maybe we can somehow make it so those exceptions get thrown into fixtures, but other ones don't? And in fact it's not even *all* `Cancelled` exceptions we want to throw in, just ones that actually belong to cancel scopes opened inside that fixture – in particular, if a fixture didn't actually open any cancel scopes, then throwing in `Cancelled` is not really cool. But it's hard to figure out which exceptions those are from the outside.

Also, we don't want cancel scopes inside fixtures to be wrapped around other fixtures or the test code, probably.. i.e. if they become cancelled it shouldn't cause other tasks to see `Cancelled` errors. Well, except if there's an unexpected error, I guess... if a fixture background task crashes, we *should* cancel the test, and maybe all the fixtures that depend on it, since the thing they were depending on is not working anymore. It seems rude to go cancelling fixtures that don't depend on it though, as opposed to letting them clean up normally. I'm not sure all fixtures are prepared for cancellation.

(Actually, there's an argument that the current `test_fixture` is too coarse-grained too, and that what we instead want is a per-fixture nursery that automatically gets cancelled and waited on at the moment when that specific fixture would be cleaned up.)

------

So.... putting all that together..... maybe there is a solution. Looking at the "workaround" above, the trick is that we push the actual fixture setup/teardown off into a separate task, so that it can't enclose anything, and it mostly can't get any exceptions that aren't its fault – the exception being the final `Cancelled` from the `test_nursery` being torn down. But that's OK, because even if it doesn't enclose the test, the nursery block still has the right *lifetime*, and that's what's really important – it means the test can use the websocket just fine.

What if we make this our general strategy for running fixtures? Every fixture gets its own task, that runs both the setup and teardown code, and we carefully arrange the lifetimes of these tasks so that we trigger the teardown code at the right moment. And... somehow manage the flow of exceptions carefully so that only the right ones go in.

I think I need a whiteboard or something to work out the details, and this is already long, so I'm going to hit post. But that's the best idea I've had so far.

I can now say for certain that the issue is not caused by the JavaScript (I reviewed that section of the code this morning). There is probably something we're both missing here. 

The thing is, the template must be served and rendered automatically by Django when placed in the correct location (it has nothing to do with MarkdownX). You are placing the file in the correct location, but it is still not rendered. All your settings, as far as I can see, appear to be correct too. 

So the only thing left here is that Django cannot locate the file for some reason. 

We can really force Django to find the file through a small hack, but that wouldn't tell us anything about the reason why the file is not served properly. 

Here is how:

**forms.py**
```python
from django import forms

from markdownx.fields import MarkdownxFormField
from markdownx.widgets import MarkdownxWidget

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

class CustomMarkdownxWidget(MarkdownxWidget):
    template_name = "path_to_your_custom_template"  # <====


class MarkdownxFormField(forms.CharField):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(MarkdownxFormField, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        if issubclass(self.widget.__class__, forms.widgets.MultiWidget):
            is_markdownx_widget = any(
                issubclass(item.__class__, CustomMarkdownxWidget)
                for item in getattr(self.widget, 'widgets', list())
            )

            if not is_markdownx_widget:
                self.widget = CustomMarkdownxWidget()

        elif not issubclass(self.widget.__class__, CustomMarkdownxWidget):
            self.widget = CustomMarkdownxWidget()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

class ForumThreadForm(forms.Form):
    content = MarkdownxFormField()

```

This is an experimental API package for building Binary Nets. We are using matrix multiplication rather than add-minus and bit-count operation at the moment. Therefore, these APIs would not speed up the inferencing, for production, you can train model via TensorLayer and deploy the model into other customized C/C++ implementation (We probably provide users an extra C/C++ binary net framework that can load model from TensorLayer).

### TODO
- For binary input, use XNOR, bit-count operation to replace matrix multiplication.
- For non binary input, use add and minus operation to replace matrix multiplication.

FWIW,  tried the sample problem and am getting the same problem on Ubuntu,  using ZeroMQ 2.1.i9.   Though I'm not sure it's really a "hard lock",   I pretty consistently get a long delay  after exactly 28233 connections.   Whether I restart the server and/or client or leave them running,  the server will be unresponsive for an period of time,  and will eventually resume with or without intervention.

So yeah, it seems as if ZeroMQ and/or PyZMQ isn't freeing up some kind of kernel resource in a timely fashion,  and new connections are delayed until that resource times out and freed by the kernel.
